questions.description: Carol has a landscape gardening business, specialising in designing, building, and maintaining gardens and outdoor areas…
questions.text: Carol has a landscape gardening business, specialising in designing, building, and maintaining gardens and outdoor areas for businesses such as hotels. In June, Carol designs and builds an outdoor area for a local businessman, Pat, for an agreed price of €10,000. Pat is required to pay Carol by July 15th. However, by the end of July Pat has still not paid Carol for her services. Carol presses for payment, to no avail. Eventually, in August Pat tells Carol that in the "current economic climate" he is not sure he will be able to pay her in full, but he offers her €6,000 in settlement of the debt. He tells her that if this offer is not accepted then nothing will be paid. Carol is worried about her cash flow, and reluctantly accepts Pat's offer. Pat pays Carol €6,000. A month later Carol changes her mind and looks for the balance, €4,000, from Pat. However, Pat says they had a binding agreement that she would accept €6,000 and she cannot now claim the €4,000. In addition, since 2016 Carol has regularly done landscaping work for a local hotel. They have no written contract, but approximately twice a month the hotel asks Carol to come in to maintain the gardens and outdoor areas. However, Carol recently learned that the hotel plans to hire a large company, Landscapes Ltd, to do this work at a cheaper rate, and that the hotel will not be asking her to come in anymore. Carol wants to know if the hotel can simply terminate their "arrangement" without notice. She thinks she should have received at least 6 months' notice of any changes given the long-term, albeit informal, relationship between them. Advise Carol.

questions.description:
Tom is a buyer for a manufacturer of tomato sauces. In April and May 2023, he was in 
negotiations with Sarah, a produce…
questions.text:
Tom is a buyer for a manufacturer of tomato sauces. In April and May 2023, he was in 
negotiations with Sarah, a producer of tomatoes based in Ireland, about the purchase of her 
entire tomato crop for the next 3 years. During a phone call in May, they agreed to a price of €2 per kilogram of tomatoes, with payment to be made on a monthly basis. During their discussion Sarah brought up the issue of the delivery schedule, and Tom said "ah we can figure that sort of detail out as we go along". Tom followed up this phone call with a text message to Sarah, in which he said: "Great to talk today! Am happy with the €2 per kilo price tag. I'll get our team here to put together the formal bit but it's great that you're on board." Sarah replies to the message with a "smiley face" emoji, as she is busy when she receives the 
text. However, the next day Sarah contacts Tom to say that she is not going to go ahead with the sale. She says that there was never a fully concluded agreement as they never agreed on the delivery schedule, and it is only an oral agreement which is not enforceable. Tom wishes to enforce the contract. In January 2022, Tom also entered into a 5 year contract with Carlos, a tomato producer in Spain. However, in the summer of 2023 the extreme weather conditions in Spain have meant that Carlos has been unable to supply the agreed quantity of tomatoes. Tom has had to buy tomatoes from another source in Northern Europe, at a more expensive contract rate, and he wants to bring an action against Carlos for breach of contract to recover the difference in price. Carlos is arguing that the contract has been frustrated by the extreme weather conditions, which have meant that the tomato crop has been reduced by 50% this year. The contract is governed by Irish law but there is no mention of what will happen if Carlos cannot supply the full amount of tomatoes due to adverse conditions. Advise Tom.

questions.description: During the summer of 2023, Mark decided to take up hiking. On 5th July 2023 he went to his local outdoor sports shop, Hi…
questions.text: During the summer of 2023, Mark decided to take up hiking. On 5th July 2023 he went to his local outdoor sports shop, Hike Ltd, and bought a raincoat described on the label as "Waterproof even under extreme weather conditions". He bought the raincoat in the sales, at a reduced price. However, when he wore the raincoat out on a hike the next weekend there was heavy rain and the coat leaked, meaning he had to abandon his hike early. When he brought the raincoat back to the shop on 15th July, the shop assistant pointed to a sign in front of the till that said "Exchange only on sale items" and said that he is not entitled to a full refund. By the end of July, poor weather means that Mark has given up on hiking, and he goes back to his favourite hobby which is playing online video games. He buys a new online game, Buzzcraft, from a software company Vendo Ltd, but from the very beginning he finds that the software glitches and won't save any of his changes or moves. When he contacts Vendo Ltd they send him a link to upload software to fix the problem, but they tell Mark that there is a €10 fee for this "upgrade". Mark wants to know if he has to pay this fee. Advise Mark on his legal rights under the Consumer Rights Act 2022, which would apply to these contracts. Briefly comment on improvements, if any, the Act has made to Mark's legal position as a consumer in these two situations.

questions.description: Ben grows hops which he sells to various independent breweries. Celine approaches Ben with a view to buying a quantity o…
questions.text: Ben grows hops which he sells to various independent breweries. Celine approaches Ben with a view to buying a quantity of hops to make her very popular beer. Celine asks if the hops have been treated with sulphur, and says that she will not even discuss the price if they have. Ben assures her that they have not. Celine is delighted and they discuss the price and delivery details. Ben then presents her with a document which he describes as "my conditions of sale". A clause in the document states as follows: "In the event of any hops sold not complying with the express terms of the contract of sale, we will refund all payments made to us by the buyer and this shall be the limit of our obligation. We hereby exclude all liability for any loss or damage arising from the use of any hops supplied by us and for any consequential loss or damage arising out of such use." Celine agrees to buy the hops and signs the document Ben has given her, but does not read through the terms. Later, after she has made the beer, Celine discovers that sulphur was used on a small portion of the hops. As a result, Celine has to dispose of her entire production of beer, suffering significant loss. When she announces her intention to sue Ben for breach of contract, he argues that the contract she signed does not mention sulphur, and that his statement about the sulphur was a mere representation and not a term of the contract. He also claims that whether it was a representation or a term, the limitation clause covers the event which has occurred and that Celine is merely entitled to a refund. Advise Celine.

questions.description: Jane is the manager of a leisure centre which is undergoing some renovations. She enters into a contract with Tony, wher…
questions.text: Jane is the manager of a leisure centre which is undergoing some renovations. She enters into a contract with Tony, whereby Tony agrees to supply and install a jacuzzi five metres long and one metre deep in the centre, at a cost of €5,000. However, when the jacuzzi is installed, Jane is annoyed because it is only four and a half metres long and ninety centimetres deep. Other than the difference in size, the jacuzzi is perfectly useable and has all the features Jane requires. Jane wants damages of €10,000 to cover the cost of tearing out the jacuzzi that has been installed and replacing it with a properly installed, bigger jacuzzi. She wants further damages for the stress and inconvenience of getting the jacuzzi replaced. Tony admits that he is in breach of contract, but he is refusing to pay damages. He says that the difference in the size of the jacuzzi is only minor, and that the jacuzzi installed is still worth at least €4,500. Jane also orders a specialised industrial floor cleaner from Supplies Ltd, to keep the floors of the swimming pool and changing rooms clean and dry. The floor cleaner is due to arrive in February 2023; however, it is only delivered in June 2023. For this time period Jane had to hire extra people to keep the floors clean and dry, as she did not have any other machine to use. She wants to sue Supplies Ltd for the losses caused as a result of the delay in delivery, but Supplies Ltd says they couldn't possibly have known that she had no other machine and that she would have to hire people to do the work manually. Advise Jane.

questions.description: Elliot is the owner of a recording studio. He rents out his recording studio to musicians for a fee of €300 per day. In …
questions.text: Elliot is the owner of a recording studio. He rents out his recording studio to musicians for a fee of €300 per day. In July 2023, he enters into a contract to rent out the studio to Fiona for the month of September, with the full fee for the month payable on 1st September. At the start of August, however, Fiona contacts Elliot to say that she will be using a different studio instead.When Elliot objects, Fiona says that she can do this because she didn't have to pay a deposit and she hadn't paid any money yet. Elliot wants advice on whether he can still insist that Fiona pay the full amount owing when it falls due and on other options that are open to him. Elliot also occasionally acts as an agent for up-and-coming musicians and singers. Three years ago, he agreed to act as an agent for Hannah, a 15 year old singer at the start of her career. The contract stated that in return for representing Hannah, Elliot was to receive a commission of 25% of all Hannah's income, from music or related merchandise, for the next 10 years. However, now that Hannah is 18 she has decided that she is unhappy with the arrangement with Elliot, and she has told Elliot that she wants to cancel their contract and look for a better deal with another agent. Elliot wants to sue Hannah for breach of contract. Advise Elliot.

questions.description: Coldstore Ltd is a transporter of refrigerated pharmaceuticals to businesses across Ireland, with its own fleet of refri…
questions.text: Coldstore Ltd is a transporter of refrigerated pharmaceuticals to businesses across Ireland, with its own fleet of refrigerated trucks to make deliveries. In January 2023 Coldstore Ltd entered into a 2 year transport and delivery contract with Merith Ltd. Term 6 of the contract stated that Coldstore Ltd would collect products from Merith Ltd every Monday and deliver them within 48 hours to specified locations across Ireland. In June 2023, however, Coldstore Ltd experienced staffing difficulties and had a shortage of drivers. For 3 consecutive weeks they collected products from Merith on Tuesday instead of Monday, and these goods were not delivered within the 48-hour window. Although Coldstore Ltd was willing to pay compensation for any losses caused as a result, Merith Ltd is unhappy with Coldstore Ltd's performance and wishes to terminate the contract as a result of these breaches of contract. Coldstore Ltd says that these were "unfortunate isolated incidents' caused by a situation which is now resolved, and it thinks that that the real reason Merith Ltd wants to terminate is to take advantage of a cheaper contract price elsewhere. Coldstore Ltd wants to know if Merith Ltd has any grounds to terminate the contract. In May 2023, Coldstore Ltd agreed to be a transporter for a large pharmaceutical company, Xyntath Ltd, with the manager Tom signing the pharmaceutical company's standard form contract. In June 2023, one of the deliveries was two days late due to the shortage of drivers. Xyntath Ltd is seeking to rely on the following clause of its contract with Coldstore Ltd: "In the event that any delivery is more than 24 hours late, the transporter shall be responsible for the costs of all and any delays in production and a payment of 150% of the cost of the material transported shall be immediately due." Coldstore Ltd believes that this is a penalty clause and should not be enforceable. Advise Coldstore Ltd.

questions.description: Which contractual principles are used by the judiciary to protect persons who provide guarantees and securities to finan…
questions.text: Which contractual principles are used by the judiciary to protect persons who provide guarantees and securities to financial institutions that advance monies to close friends and family members of the guarantor? Refer to cases in your answer.

questions.description: Jill is the manager of a creche. In January 2023 she sent an email to Supplies Ltd to enquire about purchasing a laminat…
questions.text:
Jill is the manager of a creche. In January 2023 she sent an email to Supplies Ltd to enquire about purchasing a laminator for the business. In her email she explained that the laminator would need to be “suitable to laminate large posters and artwork foe the creche, up to A2 in size”. Supplies Ltd sent her the following reply via email: “Hi Jill, For small businesses we recommend the XL Pro-Laminator. It’s perfect for bigger jobs and posters but still easy for your employees to operate. Current price is EUR150, available on our website at the link below……..” Jill clicked on the link to the Supplies Ltd website and bought the XL Pro-Laminator directly from the website. Before she entered her payment details, she ticked a box that stated that she “accepts the terms and conditions of sale” but she did not click through the link provided to read these. The laminator arrived in 2 days, but it turns out that it can only laminate objects up to A3 in size, and will not laminate anything as big as the A2 size that Jill needs. Jill is furious, as the creche operates on a tight budget and this laminator does not fulfil their needs. However, when Jill contacts Supplies Ltd to get a refund, they refuse. Supplies Ltd says that there is nothing wrong with the laminator, which works perfectly fine, and they also point to Clause 5 of their terms and conditions which states: “5: Supplies Ltd is not liable for any breach of statutory warranty of merchantability or of fitness for a particular purpose and will not issue refunds.” Advise Jill. In your answer, you can assume that Jill is acting in the course of business.
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

questions.description:
Jack is a 70 year old farmer who lives on his own in a rural area in the west of Ireland. He is
in ill health and quite …
questions.text:
Jack is a 70 year old farmer who lives on his own in a rural area in the west of Ireland. He is
in ill health and quite dependent on his neighbour, Sheila, who regularly visits him and assists him with basic household jobs such as cooking and cleaning. Sheila minds Jack’s bank account books .or him and occasionally drives him to his local bank. One day when Sheila drives Jack to his local bank Jack transfers €20,000 from his personal savings account to Sheila’s bank account. Jack does not discuss the transaction in any detail with any bank
representative; nor does he receive any independent legal advice on the transaction. Two
months later, Sheila approaches Jack and asks him to sign a petition to stop the closure of a
local school, Jack doesn’t have his reading glasses and so he signs the petition without
reading it. Jack’s nephew Colm visits Jack later that year and upon investigation discovers that the
“petition” is, in fact, a bank guarantee form which guarantees Sheila's debts up to €25,000. Colm is concerned that his uncle may become liable under this guarantee and wants to know if there is any way the guarantee may be void. He also wants to know if the transfer of €20,000 to Sheila can be set aside. Advise Colm.

questions.description:
Sorcha is a world-famous Irish actress. In October 2022 she agrees with the film company
Irish Movies Ltd to play the le…
questions.text:
Sorcha is a world-famous Irish actress. In October 2022 she agrees with the film company
Irish Movies Ltd to play the lead role in a film called “The Rings of the banshee”. She is to receive a payment of €2 million for making the film. Once Sorcha signs the contract, Irish
Movies Ltd incurs substantial costs in getting ready for production. In particular, it spends €750,000 on producing a movie set and a further €250,000 on securing special effects experts. However, shortly after signing the contract, Sorcha informs Irish Movies Ltd that she will no longer be appearing in the film, saying that she wants to take her career in a different direction. Irish Movies Ltd decides that without Sorcha there’s no point going ahead with the film, and
therefore cancels production. They predict that if the. film had gone ahead with Sorcha as
planned that they would have made profits of €5 million. They now wish to sue Sorcha for their lost profits. and for their wasted expenditure. However, Sorcha says that Irish Movies Ltd could easily have hired another actress to replace her:, and that the real reason they cancelled production was that the film script for “The Rings of the Banshee” was so terrible that they would not have made any profit from the film had it gone ahead. Advise Sorcha.

questions.description:
Todd is the manager of a large construction company, Construction Ltd. He needs an XL
Super Crane for his latest project…
questions.text:
Todd is the manager of a large construction company, Construction Ltd. He needs an XL
Super Crane for his latest project, so he emails Sarah, the manager of Plant Ltd, to enquire
about hiring an XL Super Crane for a 2-year period. Sarah emails the following response to Todd: “Hi Todd, we are happy to offer the XL Super Crane for a 2 year lease, at a rate of €5,090 per month payable in monthly instalments, plus an initial delivery charge of €1,000. This offer is subject to Plant Ltd.'s standard terms and conditions, which are attached to this email.” Term 6 of Plant Ltd.’s standard terms and conditions was a price variation clause which provided that the rent could be increased by up to 10% during the course of the hire period, in the event of changes in market conditions. Todd likes the price that is offered, but isn’t happy with Term 6 of the standard terms and
conditions. He edits the terms and conditions that Sarah has sent him by deleting Term 6. He then replies by email to Sarah, saying “That's great Sarah, I’m happy with the attached terms.Can you deliver next week?” The attached terms and conditions look exactly like the ones Sarah originally sent, but without Term 6. Sarah replies saying “That's perfect, we will organize delivery for next week.” She does not notice that the terms and conditions attached to the email are different from the ones she originally sent out and does not refer to this in her email. The XL Super Crane is delivered in January, and invoices are sent in January and February
for €5,000 per month. However, in March, Sarah sends an invoice for €5,500, referring to a change in market conditions and Term 6 of the contract as an explanation for the increase in
price. Todd is refusing to pay the increased price. He does not deny that ..ere has been a
change in market conditions, but he argues that Term 6 is not pat of i. > contract and was
never agreed to by him. Advise Todd.

questions.description: Ross runs a transport company. On 6 January 2023, Ross enters into a contract with Marie, who runs a chain of supermarke…
questions.text:
Ross runs a transport company. On 6 January 2023, Ross enters into a contract with Marie, who runs a chain of supermarkets across Ireland. The contract provides that Ross will transport frozen goods from Dublin Port to Marie’s supermarkets once a month from February 2023 to February 2024. Payment is to be monthly, with the first payment of €500 due on 28" February 2023. However, in mid-January Ross does a review of his business. He calculates that he will be performing this contract at a loss,  and he would be better off entering a transport agreement with someone else at a higher rate per month. He calls Marie to cancel the agreement. Marie is furious, as she says they had a binding contract and it will now be more expensive to hire another transporter. Ross thinks he can escape the deal as Marie has not yet paid any money or any deposit. Ross also has an ongoing  arrangement with a kitchen unit manufacturer, Kitchens Ltd, to transport kitchen units and kitchen equipment to different locations around the country. Whenever Kitchen Ltd needs something transported, the manager Carla simply phones Ross
with the details told Ross organises the transport. This has happened approximately twice a month for the past two years, although there is no written contract in place. However, Carla
has recently told Ross that she is not going to continue placing orders with him. Ross believes that an implied contract exists because of their ongoing relationship, and that Carla cannot simply cancel this contract. He believes that this implied contract includes an implied duty of good faith that requires Kitchens Ltd/Carla to give reasonable notice of six months to terminate the contract. Advise Ross.

questions.description: Niamh is an internationally recognised collector of vintage books and manuscripts. She attends a book fair where she mee…
questions.text:
Niamh is an internationally recognised collector of vintage books and manuscripts. She attends a book fair where she meets Sean, who is selling a selection of books and notebooks,
Sean explains to her that his grandfather was the famous author Peter McClavey, and he is
selling a collection of books and notebooks that‘he found in his grandfather's attic. Niamh inspects the books at Sean’s stand and is delighted to find a set of notebooks with what looks like handwritten notes relating to Peter McClavey’s most famsus book,, Times of our Lives. Niamh exclaims that the notes would be “great for research!” and Sean says he can sell them to her for €5,000. However, Sean does not admit that although the notebooks came from his grandfather's attic, they were not in fact those of the author Peter McClavey, but rather were written by his brother, Paul McClavey after the publication of Times of our Lives. When Niamh tells Sean sympathetically that it must be hard to part with his grandfather’s work, Sean responds merely by saying “No, it is not”. Niamh buys notebooks for €5,000. Six months later when Niamh tries to sell the notebooks to a university, she discovers that
they were not in fact written by Peter McClavey. She claims that either the contract with Sean is invalid as a result of this mistake and that she is entitled to her money back, or that Sean is liable for failing to disclose the fact that the notebooks were not in fact written by his grandfather. Advise Niamh.

questions.description:
Sam, Tom and Bob are friends who meet every Thursday night for a few drinks in their local
pub. One week, after four pin…
questions.text:
Sam, Tom and Bob are friends who meet every Thursday night for a few drinks in their local
pub. One week, after four pints of beer each, Sam says that he saw that the National Lottery
Jackpot will be €5 million the  following Saturday, and asks if the others would be interested in “going in together“ to buy multiple tickets to increase the chances of winning. Sam suggests that if they each contribute €40 they will be able to buy €120 worth of tickets. Tom immediately agrees and gives Sam €40 to buy tickets. Bob says he doesn’t have cash on him, but that he will transfer money ever to Sam “as soon as he has a chance”. Sam goes to the shop the next day and buys €20 worth of Lottery tickets. Bob forgets to transfer the money over to Sam, and only has a vague recollection of the entire conversation. It turns out that one of the tickets Sam bought has the winning numbers. However, Sam now wants to keep the full €5 million, and doesn’t want to pay out money to either Tom or Bob. He says it wasn’t a serious or legally enforceable agreement, as it was made between friends after a few drinks. In addition, he says that Bob didn’t even pay his share of the money. Tom and Bob are threatening to bring legal action for their share of the winnings. Advise Sam.

questions.description: "It is possible to vary an existing contractual relationship in the absence of fresh consideration, on the basis of prom…
questions.text: "It is possible to vary an existing contractual relationship in the absence of fresh consideration, on the basis of promissory estoppel. However strict limitations apply.” Discuss this statement with reference to case law. |In your view, is the current law satisfactory or should it be easier - or more difficult - to modify an existing contract?

questions.description: Mega Ltd sells equipment and machinery to the farms and agricultural businesses. In January 2020 Mega Ltd entered into a…
questions.text:
Mega Ltd sells equipment and machinery to the farms and agricultural businesses. In January 2020 Mega Ltd entered into a 5 year transport agreement with Trucks Ltd, under which Trucks Ltd agreed to deliver machinery and equipment to Mega Ltd's customers at a fixed rate of €5 per kilometre. However, by August 2022, unanticipated high rates of inflation and an extraordinary rise in fuel prices had greatly  increased the cost to Trucks Ltd of transporting the goods. Trucks Ltd says it needs to increase its rate to €8 a kilometre, as it says that it cannot continue to suffer losses transporting the goods. Trucks Ltd says that if Mega Ltd does not agree to the increase in price that they will be forced to terminate the contract. Mega Ltd seeks your advice on whether Trucks Ltd can increase its rates in this 
manner and/or terminate the contract. In addition, in January 2022 Mega Ltd entered into a contract for the sale of machinery 
worth €500,000 to a large farm in Ruritania, with €50,000 being paid in advance. However, Ruritania has since become involved in an international conflict and is the subject of sanctions issued by the EU which mean that it is now illegal to trade with any business entity in Ruritania in a number of sectors, including agriculture. Mega Ltd has contacted the buyers in Ruritania to say that they cannot go ahead with the contract. The buyers have threatened to sue for breach of contract and are also looking for the immediate return of the €50,000 paid in advance. Advise Mega Ltd. In your answer, assume that the contracts are governed by Irish law.

questions.description:
Sarah owns a horse riding school. In May 2022 she saw the following advertisement for a 
horse on the website "GreatDeal…
questions.text:
Sarah owns a horse riding school. In May 2022 she saw the following advertisement for a 
horse on the website "GreatDeal.ie": "Quiet grey horse for sale. 9 years old, in good health. Great with children and 
nervous riders. Private seller." Sarah contacted Tom, the owner of the horse, and arranged a viewing. Sarah explained 
that she was interested in the horse to use in her riding school with children and beginners. 
During the viewing the horse became excited and bucked, but Tom stated that it would "quickly settle into any new environment". When Sarah suggested that she would have a 
vet examine the horse, Tom said there was "no need" as he had had "no issues at all". Sarah bought the horse. However, even after a settling period of a few weeks it became clear that the horse was not quiet, and would try to buck off even experienced riders. In addition, the horse quickly developed a limp and when Sarah brought it to the vets the vet said that the horse had a pre• existing injury which was causing the limp. The horse was entirely unsuitable for Sarah's business and she wants to return it and get her money back. However, Tom  says that Sarah had an opportunity to view the horse and took it "as seen". In addition, he says that as he is a private seller, and not operating in the course of business, she has no rights against him. Advise Sarah. Would your answer be any different if Tom was acting in the course of 
business rather than as a private seller?

questions.description:
In January 2019, Niamh rents an office from Clumbo Ltd, agreeing to pay an annual rent 
of €58,000 subject to a rent rev…
questions.text:
In January 2019, Niamh rents an office from Clumbo Ltd, agreeing to pay an annual rent 
of €58,000 subject to a rent review in June 2022. The rent review clause provides that on that date the rent should be increased to the current market rental value as agreed between the parties. In June 2022, Max, a senior manager in Clumbo Ltd, writes to Niamh suggesting a figure of €57,000 for the new rent. Niamh writes back readily agreeing to renew upon the terms proposed. Max then realises his mistake and writes to Niamh explaining that he had intended to propose a rent of €75,000. Niamh informs Max that she will not agree to the correction proposed and that she considers that a binding agreement exists. While accepting that Max has authority to act on its behalf, Clumbo Ltd argues that Max's mistake invalidates the agreement. Clumbo Ltd is unhappy with Max's performance and it decides his annual salary of €90,000 could be put to better use. In July 2022, Max is dismissed and Clumbo Ltd pays him compensation of €150,000. However it is subsequently discovered that Max has engaged in insider dealing in breach of his service contract and in breach of company law. It becomes clear thus that at the time it terminated his contract, Clumbo Ltd could have dismissed Max for breach without having to pay compensation. Clumbo Ltd argues that the termination contract is void and the compensation repayable. Advise Clumbo Ltd. 1

questions.description: Becky is the owner of a small hotel. She needs the light and electricity sockets in multiple  rooms fixed, and as she is…
questions.text: Becky is the owner of a small hotel. She needs the light and electricity sockets in multiple  rooms fixed, and as she is on a tight budget she asks the local electrician, Mark, if he can "do a deal". Mark admits that he is not a Registered Electrical Contractor, but he will do the job cheaply for Becky if she pays him cash in hand in advance. Becky pays Mark €1,000, but Mark only completes the work in half of the rooms. In addition, some of the electricity sockets are not working properly and Becky is worried that these might be a fire hazard. Becky contacts Mark to get her money back, and to ask him to fix the damaged sockets. Mark says that as it is an offence under the Electricity Regulation Act 1999 for any individual to carry out electrical works unless they are a Registered Electrical Contractor the contract is illegal and void. He refuses to pay back Becky the money she paid him. Advise Becky.

questions.description: Dervla is a graphic designer who is concerned about health issues, in particular the dangers of young people smoking. On…
questions.text: Dervla is a graphic designer who is concerned about health issues, in particular the dangers of young people smoking. On her own initiative she designs a poster for "Tobac", a charity that works to educate young people about the dangers of smoking. Edward, the public relations officer for Tobac, is delighted with the poster, and in an email to Dervla he writes: "We love your poster! As a token of our gratitude for your hard work, we would like to give you €1,000." Dervla replies "That's great, thank you!" However, the next week Edward emails Dervla to say that owing to cutbacks they are no longer able to pay her any money. Dervla wants to know if there is any way she can claim the €1,000 promised. Dervla discovers that her 15 year old niece Anna has started smoking. Dervla tells Anna that if she quits smoking and does not smoke for six months that she will pay her €500. Six months later Anna contacts Dervla to let her know that she has successfully abstained from smoking for six months and that she wants her money. Dervla argues that she wasn't serious when she offered Anna the money; that she has not benefited from Anna's actions; and that it was only a promise to a young family member and not legally binding. She wants to know if she has to pay Anna the €500. Advise Dervla.

questions.description:
Brian is the manager of Viewplex Cinema, a cinema in Galway. On 1st March 2022, Brian 
entered into a contract with Andy…
questions.text:
Brian is the manager of Viewplex Cinema, a cinema in Galway. On 1st March 2022, Brian 
entered into a contract with Andy, the manager of the local leisure centre and swimming pool, to display advertisements for the leisure centre in the Viewplex Cinema building for a period of one year. Under the terms of the contract, Viewplex Cinema is to display the advertisements for one year from 2nd May 2022, and Andy is to pay in one lump sum of €5,000 at the end of the agreed period. On 2nd April 2022, Andy tells Brian that he no longer wants to use Viewplex Cinema for advertisements, because he has found another location willing to display the advertisements at a lower price. However, Brian insists on continuing with the contract and displays the advertisements in the cinema from 2nd May as stipulated in the contract. Brian says that he is going to perform the contract as agreed and claim the full lump sum at the end of the year. Andy is furious, as he says that Brian could easily find another business owner willing to display advertisements in the cinema. He says that Brian should not be able to claim the full €5,000 due as he has failed to mitigate his losses. Advise Andy.

questions.description: Sophie is the owner of a restaurant. With the increased business after the easing of Covid restrictions she decides to e…
questions.text:
Sophie is the owner of a restaurant. With the increased business after the easing of Covid restrictions she decides to expand her business premises. In January 2022, she hires Pat to build a fully completed extension onto the restaurant, including a bigger kitchen and washroom. Pat then enters into a sub-contract with Jim, a local plumber, to carry out the plumbing work on the new extension. The contract between Sophie and Pat clearly states that the work was to be finished by May 31st 2022. However, owing to staff shortages there are significant delays in finishing the work and Sophie is only able to open up part of her restaurant during the busy tourist season. Sophie has lost out on the increased profits she was expecting on completion of the extension, and wants to know if she can claim damages. She notes that her contract seems to allow for this possibility, as clause 12 states: "Time is of the essence of this contract. If the contractor fails to complete its work by the date specified, then the contractor hereby agrees to pay to the client, as liquidated clauses? Is this protection adequate or in need of reform? Refer to relevant case law and legislation in your answer damages and not as penalty, the sum of €500 per day for each calendar day beyond the specified completion date during which the contractor fails to complete the work." Sophie says that she used a template contract she found on the internet, but that she changed the sum to €500 as she felt that was a fair estimation of what she would lose if the work was delayed. Pat is arguing that he was unaware of the term, and says it is an unfair contract term and not enforceable. In addition, when the work is complete Sophie is unhappy with the quality of some of Jim's plumbing work. She says that the water pressure is too low for the dishwashers to work 
effectively and that there is a major leak from one of the sinks. She wants to sue Jim for breach of contract. Advise Sophie.

questions.description: How are Irish consumers protected from unfair contract terms and exclusion
questions.text: How are Irish consumers protected from unfair contract terms and exclusion

questions.description:
Ken is a dairy farmer with a 5 years lease on a 20 acre piece of farm land, consisting of fields 
and animal sheds with …
questions.text:
Ken is a dairy farmer with a 5 years lease on a 20 acre piece of farm land, consisting of fields 
and animal sheds with machines to milk the cows. When he entered into the lease he explained to the landlord, Mary, that he had chosen this land as it was “so close to the local dairy” that it would be perfect for his dairy business and would greatly reduce his transport costs. However, one year into the lease there was an unprecedented outbreak of disease in livestock in the local area. This was declared an animal health crisis, and all farms in the area, including Ken’s, were closed down for a period of 6 months by the local health authority. During this time, Ken had no income from his cow herd and, although a small amount of compensation was paid by the local health authority, he was unable to pay the rent on the lease. Mary is threatening to sue Ken for the rent owed for the 6 months his farm was closed, and Ken wants to know if he has any defence to her claim for rent. In addition, the public health crisis meant that the local dairy was forced to close. This means that Ken now has to travel a much farther distance to sell the milk, making his business economically unviable for the future. Although there are no clauses in the contract dealing with the possibility of early termination of the lease, Ken is considering terminating his lease early, and wants to know if he might have any grounds for doing so. Advise Ken.

questions.description: Dervla is a freelance graphic designer. She enters into a contract with a national newspaper to design the advertisement…
questions.text: Dervla is a freelance graphic designer. She enters into a contract with a national newspaper to design the advertisements for the weekend edition of its newspaper, for a period of 2 years. During negotiations, she states that the nature of the job means that she will be able to do it from home. The newspaper editor says "Of course! We wouldn't expect you to come into the office!" However, six months into the contract, the newspaper editor changes. The new editor says that he wants Dervla to come into the newspaper offices every Thursday to do the design work, instead of working from home. When Dervla says that she cannot do so, he terminates the contract "for breach of contract". Dervla is furious, as she is losing out on a major source of income. She wants to know if she has any cause of action in contract law. Advise Dervla.

questions.description: Coldstore Ltd is a transporter of frozen food and pharmaceuticals to businesses across Ireland, with its own fleet of re…
questions.text: Coldstore Ltd is a transporter of frozen food and pharmaceuticals to businesses across Ireland, with its own fleet of refrigerated trucks to make deliveries. In January 2021, Coldstore Ltd agreed to be a transporter for a large pharmaceutical company, Xyn Ltd. Tom, the manager of Coldstore Ltd, signed the pharmaceutical company's standard form contract. In December 2021, one of the deliveries was two days late due to a shortage of drivers. Xyn Ltd claims that, as a result of this delay, one of their most profitable production lines, producing Covid vaccines for the Government, was held up, losing them profits of €1 million. Xyn Ltd is seeking damages for lost profits, based on the following clause of its contract with Coldstore Ltd: "In the event that any delivery is more than 24 hours late, the transporter shall be responsible for the costs of all and any delays in production, including all lost profits, and an additional payment of 150% of the cost of the material transported shall be immediately due." Coldstore Ltd believes that this is a penalty clause and should not be enforceable. In addition, Coldstore Ltd says that they had no way of knowing that that particular delivery was so important and would result in this extent of lost profits if delayed. Advise Coldstore Ltd.

questions.description: Carla is a dance teacher who has recently set up a dance school, StarShine. She also has a website on which she provides…
questions.text:
Carla is a dance teacher who has recently set up a dance school, StarShine. She also has a website on which she provides information about her classes and sells merchandise such as dance clothes, ballet shoes and equipment. Before her students take their first class she asks 
them (or their parents) to provide their details and sign a document that she wrote using a 
template she found on the internet. The document contains the following clauses: "1. All classes are taken at students' own risk. Starshine is not liable for any personal injury which is caused during our classes, even if caused by negligence of Starshine instructors or employees. All statutory warranties and conditions are excluded. 2. The class fee for the term, specified overleaf, must be paid in full before term begins, and is non-refundable, including in the event that a class is cancelled for any reason. An administrative fee of €30 is payable if payment is not made on time." She is now worried about the validity of these clauses and seeks your advice. In addition, a pair of ballet shoes purchased on her website was recently returned by a customer who claimed that "they didn't fit", even though the shoes were the size stated on the website. She says that on her website it is clearly stated that she has a "no returns unless faulty" policy, and wants to know if she has to refund this customer, who returned the goods one week after purchase. Advise Carla.

questions.description: Fiona is a landscape gardener, who specialises in designing, building and maintaining gardens and outdoor areas for busi…
questions.text: Fiona is a landscape gardener, who specialises in designing, building and maintaining gardens and outdoor areas for businesses such as hotels. Fiona receives a phone call from a local mushroom grower, Mark, asking if Fiona would be interested in taking a truck full of mushroom compost for use in her business, as otherwise he "would just be throwing it out". Fiona is delighted and immediately agrees to take the compost. When she collects the compost the next day Fiona is so happy with it that she promises she will pay Mark €200. Mark is delighted by this; however, Fiona never pays him the money. Mark is now insisting that she owes him the €200. During the Covid pandemic, Fiona realises that she needs additional financial support if the business is to succeed. She enters into an agreement with Thomas, a local entrepreneur who sells plants, bushes and gardening equipment. Thomas agrees to invest €30,000 in Fiona’s business in return fir a promise that Fiona will enter into a contract with him for the supply of plants and gardening equipment to her business for the next year.However, after two weeks of discussions, they fail to agree on important terms of the supply contract, including the price and delivery arrangements. Fiona wants to end negotiations, but Thomas is insisting that she is under a contractual duty to negotiate the contract and/or has a duty of good faith to negotiate. Advise Fiona.

questions.description: Ross is the manager of a construction site. He needs to hire a large excavator for the site. On Monday he emails Trina, …
questions.text: Ross is the manager of a construction site. He needs to hire a large excavator for the site. On Monday he emails Trina, the owner of "Exc Hire Ltd" to enquire about the price of hiring an "XL Excavator" for six months. That afternoon, Trina responds as follows: "Thank you for your enquiry. We have one XL Excavator in stock, at a price of €3,000 per month plus delivery charges. If interested, please fill out the attached order form and return within 3 working days." On Wednesday Ross replies, saying "That's great, I'll take it!" However, he does not include  the order form with his email. Trina replies to tell him that she hired out the XL Excavator to  somebody else on Tuesday and so she cannot hire it to Ross. Ross is angry because he cannot find the XL Excavator for hire at this price anywhere else, and he believes he has a binding contract with Trina. Advise Ross. Would your answer be different if Ross had filled in and returned the order form as requested?

questions.description: Shady Ltd operates a large manufacturing plant producing a range of popular breakfast cereals. Shady Ltd was recently in…
questions.text: Shady Ltd operates a large manufacturing plant producing a range of popular breakfast cereals. Shady Ltd was recently invoiced by its accountants, Audit Ltd, for €14,000 for audit services provided. Shady Ltd’s managing director, Tom, has seen a rumour on social media that Audit Ltd is in tough financial situation and is at risk of becoming insolvent. Deciding to take advantage of this, he tells Audit ltd that unless the quoted figure of €14,000 is substantially reduced, he “won’t pay them a penny” and he will personally see to it that no company in the country will ever hire them again. Audit Ltd agrees to a payment of €10,000 but subsequently sends a letter seeking the full €14,000 due, arguing that the agreement to pay €10,000 is void for duress. Advise Shady Ltd. Are there any other legal doctrines that Audit Ltd could potentially rely on, in addition to duress?

questions.description: Charm Ltd is a firm of computer software engineers, which specialises in providing protection to businesses against cybe…
questions.text:
Charm Ltd is a firm of computer software engineers, which specialises in providing protection to businesses against cyber-attacks, malicious software and online viruses and scams. In September 2021, Charm Ltd entered into a 5-year contract with Dart Ltd, to provide 
computer protection and maintenance services for a monthly fee of €10,000. The contract states (amongst other things): "Term 5: Charm Ltd will use its best endeavours to provide and ensure continuous supply of protection against cyber-attacks and malicious software for the duration of the agreement." However, on three separate occasions in December 2021 Dart Ltd was the victim of small-
scale cyber-attacks. On each occasion the harm was quickly discovered and rectified, but Dart Ltd is now concerned that Charm Ltd may not be able to keep them safe. Dart Ltd has said that it wants to terminate its contract with Charm Ltd based on a breach of Term 5. In January 2022 Charm Ltd entered into a 2 year contract with another company, Arrow Ltd, to provide a similar range of services. The contract with Arrow Ltd was to begin in May 2022, with the first monthly payment due that month. However, in February 2022 Arrow Ltd purported to cancel the contract, saying that they were entitled to do so as performance had not begun and they had given "reasonable notice" approximately two months before the first payment was due. Advise Charm Ltd on both contracts.

questions.description: Dervla decides to go on a trip around Ireland in a camper van. She sees the following advertisement on a website: “Secon…
questions.text: Dervla decides to go on a trip around Ireland in a camper van. She sees the following advertisement on a website: “Second-hand camper van for sale. All offers over €6,000 considered. If interested, please email LauraK@email.ie”. On Monday Dervla sends Laura an email and the following e-mail conversation follows: Dervla: “Hi! I'm interested in the camper van but I can pay €5,000 max” Laura: “Sorry I can't sell it for less than €6,000.” Dervla: “Can you hold it for me and give me a few days to think about it?” Laura: “Okay, if you really want I can hold on and not sell to anyone else for a few days. Can you get back to me by Saturday at the latest with a decision?” Dervla: “Great thank you!” On Friday, Dervla emails Laura to say she will pay €6,000 “as agreed”. However, Laura tells Dervla that she already sold the camper van to another individual, Ben, that morning and so cannot sell it to Dervla. Dervla wants to now if she has entered into a binding contract for the sale of the camper van and whether he has a cause of action against Laura for selling the camper van to Ben before Saturday. Advise Dervla.

questions.description: Tech Ltd has a website on which it sells computers, tablets and other electronic devices in Ireland. Last month an error…
questions.text:
Tech Ltd has a website on which it sells computers, tablets and other electronic devices in Ireland. Last month an error was made by an employee when updating the website. As a result, a high-end computer which is normally sold for €5,000 was put on sale on the website for €500 for approximately 2 hours before the mistake was realised and rectified. 
However, in that time word had spread on social media about the “great offer” on the website, and approximately 200 customers had ordered the computer at the lower price. Each customer had received an automated email from Tech Ltd to say that the order had been received, although no money was taken from any customer's credit card or bank account at this stage. Tech Ltd does not want to sell the computers at such a low price, but angry customers have phoned the customer care line and have gone on social media to insist that they have a contractual right to the computer at the lower price. Tech Ltd asserts that no contract has been formed or alternatively, that if it has, that Tech made a very clear 
mistake as to the price and so should not have to sell at that low price. Advise Tech Ltd. Would your answer be different if the mistake was not on a website but rather on a price tag in a physical shop, with only one customer attempting to purchase the computer before the mistake is realised?

questions.description: Siopa Ltd is a major supermarket chain which is interested in opening a large new supermarket in a new shopping centre i…
questions.text: Siopa Ltd is a major supermarket chain which is interested in opening a large new supermarket in a new shopping centre in Ashboume, Co. Meath. The shopping centre is owned by Centre Ltd and contains space for thirty other shops or businesses. After extensive negotiations overseen by teams of commercial solicitors, Siopa Ltd enters into a 10 year lease with Centre Ltd. Under clause 6 of the contract Centre Ltd agrees “to make best efforts to ensure that at least 90% of the centre units are filled at all times with tenants”. However, after one year of the lease Siopa Ltd is unhappy with low levels of business in the supermarket. Siopa Ltd believes this is partly due to the lack of other appealing shops and businesses in the centre. The shopping centre is only 50% full, and moreover Siopa Ltd feels that some of the businesses operating in the centre are of poor quality and not able to attract customers to the shopping centre. Siopa Ltd feels that Centre Ltd has not made appropriate efforts to obtain tenants — although it has advertised the vacant units in national newspapers, it has not actively approached potential tenants individually to invite them to lease a unit. Siopa Ltd also feels that the word “tenants” in clause 6 should be interpreted to mean “high quality” tenants, either by means of interpretation of the contract or by implication. Siopa Ltd says this is proven by Centre Ltd’s own advertisements stating that “high quality tenants” are sought. Siopa Ltd is threatening to terminate the lease for breach of contract because it says Centre Ltd has not met its obligations in clause 6. However, Centre Ltd says that it has made “best efforts” to obtain tenants by advertising the vacant units, and that it could not be expected to do more. Centre Ltd also argues that the contract cannot be interpreted as meaning that "high quality' tenants must be found, either expressly or by implication. Advise Centre Ltd.

questions.description: In 2018 Pub Enterprises Ltd (PE Ltd) entered into a 10 year lease with Max, the owner of a large licensed premises. The …
questions.text:
In 2018 Pub Enterprises Ltd (PE Ltd) entered into a 10 year lease with Max, the owner of a large licensed premises. The lease stated that the premises was only to be used as a pub/bar. However, in March 2020, owing to Government restrictions during the Covid pandemic, the pub was forced to close and PE Ltd struggled to pay the agreed rent. Max 
agreed to accept a reduced rent from PE Ltd, “for as long as the Government restrictions due to Covid continue”. There was no consideration for the promise to accept a lower rent, nor was the promise formalised in a deed under seal. By August 2021 the restrictions were partially lifted, but PE Ltd’s business was still operating at only 50% of its usual capacity. However, Max is claiming that the full rent rate should resume, and moreover is now claiming payment of the unpaid portion of the original rate of rent for the period from March 2020 to July 2021. Advise PE Ltd.

questions.description: Travel Ltd is a travel agent that specialises in cruises around Europe, and exclusively sells cruise tickets on behalf o…
questions.text:
Travel Ltd is a travel agent that specialises in cruises around Europe, and exclusively sells cruise tickets on behalf of European Cruises Ltd (EC Ltd). The contract between Travel Ltd and EC Ltd states that EC Ltd will allocate 200 tickets to Travel Ltd each month, and pay commission for tickets sold. The contract can be terminated by EC Ltd at one month's notice. This arrangement ran successfully for five years. However, a dispute recently arose whereby Travel Ltd alleged that EC Ltd had failed to pay the correct amount of commission. EC Ltd denied these claims, and gave notice that it would terminate the arrangement between them by giving one month's notice. This would have put Travel Ltd out of business. 
EC Ltd then offered to enter into a new contract with Travel Ltd. This new contract included a new term by which Travel Ltd waived any claims it might have for any previously unpaid commission. When Travel Ltd's manager was shown this contract he expressed doubts about this new term, but EC Ltd refused his request to take a copy of the contract with him in order to discuss it and obtain legal advice. Travel Ltd entered into the new contract. Travel Ltd now wants to bring a claim against EC Ltd for the unpaid commission. It wants to know whether the new contract it entered into can be set aside on the basis that it was entered into under economic duress. Advise Travel Ltd.

questions.description: Transport Shipping Ltd hired a ship to Grains Ltd in June for a hire fee of €2,000 per day. The ship was due to be retur…
questions.text: Transport Shipping Ltd hired a ship to Grains Ltd in June for a hire fee of €2,000 per day. The ship was due to be returned on 31st August and so Transport Shipping Ltd arranged to hire the same ship out to a third party, McCann Ltd, for 6 months from 1st September. Transport Shipping Ltd was able to negotiate a hire fee of €4,000 per day for this second hire period with McCann Ltd. This was a particularly high fee which Transport Shipping Ltd was able to negotiate as its ship had unique characteristics which McCann Ltd required. Grains Ltd failed to return the ship until 30th September. Grains Ltd admits it is in breach of contract and is willing to pay damages to cover an extra month's hire at the contract rate of €2,000 per day (a total of €60,000). As a result of the late delivery of the ship, however, Transport Shipping Ltd was forced to renegotiate its contract with McCann Ltd to allow for a delayed start to the hire period and Transport Shipping Ltd suffered losses of €200,000 as a result. Grains Ltd says that it should not be liable for these losses as it was unaware of the particularly high fee which Transport Ltd had negotiated. However, Grains Ltd does admit that it was aware that it was likely that other contracts would be impacted by the delayed return of the ship. Transport Shipping Ltd wants to know what damages it might be able to claim under general contract law. Advise Transport Shipping Ltd.

questions.description: Lucy runs a public relations business called Publicity Ltd. Just over three months ago Lucy entered into a 2-year writte…
questions.text:
Lucy runs a public relations business called Publicity Ltd. Just over three months ago Lucy entered into a 2-year written contract with a small business, Instant Fame Ltd (IF Ltd), under which IF Ltd agreed to manage the social media aspects of Lucy's business. A fee is payable monthly. During pre-contract discussions, Claude, the owner of IF Ltd, told Lucy that he had “successfully managed social media accounts for several Irish celebrities, including Sile Shardashion”. However, three months into the contract, Lucy meets Sile Shardashion. Sile says that while Claude did manage her social media account it was only for a few weeks and she had to fire him as he made a number of serious errors, as a result of which she lost followers on several social media platforms. Lucy is furious at Claude for misleading her like this. She wishes to cancel her contract with Claude and seeks your advice. In addition, in June Lucy entered into a I-year written contract with Max to design and maintain her business website. Performance was due to begin on September 1st, with payment due every month. However, in July Lucy decided that Max was a bit expensive, and she wanted to cancel the contract with him and hire someone else instead. She believes 
that as Max hasn't commenced work and she hasn't paid a deposit or other payment, that she should be able to cancel this contract. Advise Lucy.

questions.description: Answer (a) or (b) Discuss how exemption clauses are controlled by legislation in Ireland. What impact has the European U…
questions.text:
Answer (a) or (b) Discuss how exemption clauses are controlled by legislation in Ireland. What impact has the European Union had on the regulation of exemption clauses and unfair terms? 
 
Or Discuss the approach of the Irish courts to penalty clauses in contracts.

questions.description: Jack wants to sell his car, so on Monday he puts an advertisement up on the car selling website, carsforsale.ie, which s…
questions.text: Jack wants to sell his car, so on Monday he puts an advertisement up on the car selling website, carsforsale.ie, which states: "Private Sale: Renault Paguna, 2013. 5 door, Diesel, 100,000km. In excellent condition. €10,000". That evening he receives an email from Paula, who says "I'm very interested in your car. Would you accept €9,000 for it?" Jack immediately replies, stating "Sorry, I'm only willing to sell for €10,000." On Tuesday he receives an email from Sam, who is willing to pay the full €10,000 for the car. Jack is delighted and immediately replies to confirm the sale. Sam takes the car for a test drive that evening, pays the full €10,000 and takes the car. On Wednesday, Jack receives another email from Paula, which states: "Okay, I'll take it for €10,000." When Jack replies to say he has sold the car to Sam, Paula is furious. She insists that they have a contract and that Jack has to sell her the car. Four weeks after the car has been sold to Sam, Jack receives another email from Sam. In this email, Sam complains about the condition of the car, saying that Jack never told him that the clutch was in need of repairs costing €900. He says that the car is not as advertised and not of merchantable quality, and wants Jack to pay for the repairs. Advise Jack.

questions.description: Fred operates a plant hire business, hiring out bulky machinery for the construction and demolition of buildings and roa…
questions.text: Fred operates a plant hire business, hiring out bulky machinery for the construction and demolition of buildings and roads. He leases a site from Maire intending to use it for storing and recycling the machinery and materials. The 5 year lease provides that Fred may "use the premises only for the storage and recycling of machinery and bulk road materials or for such other purposes as may from time to time be approved in writing by the Landlord". After using this site for one month, noise and dust from his operations lead to complaints. The local council serves an enforcement notice on Fred and Maire, ordering them to cease using the site for the recycling of construction and demolition materials on the grounds that this violates existing planning laws. Fred claims that the lease has been frustrated as the order renders illegal the only use of the site which was permitted under the lease and/or the only use of relevance to him. He claims that the property is of no value to him now and that the likelihood of sub-leasing the site is minimal. Advise Fred.

questions.description: John owns a restaurant.  He wishes to obtain a loan of €150,000 from his bank to add an extra dining room and larger kit…
questions.text: John owns a restaurant.  He wishes to obtain a loan of €150,000 from his bank to add an extra dining room and larger kitchen to the restaurant premises. His wife Sally inherited a house in Dublin city from her aunt, and John tells Sally that he needs her to act as a guarantor for the loan and to charge her interest in the house as security for the loan. Sally agrees to this as she is worried about their marriage and is worried John will leave her. When Sally is in the bank, she is asked to sign a form which states that she has received dependent advice from a solicitor, but in fact she has not received any such legal advice. When she queries signing the form, the bank manager states "Oh it's just a formality. We never enforce these things anyway. You can go talk to your solicitor about it if you're worried though." Sally signs, but does not consult her solicitor at any point. By November 2020 John is in serious difficulties. He has failed to pay any of his loan repayments and Sally is worried that the bank will take possession of her house. Advise Sally.

questions.description: Tracey runs a business manufacturing and selling skin care creams made from ethically produced ingredients in eco-friend…
questions.text: Tracey runs a business manufacturing and selling skin care creams made from ethically produced ingredients in eco-friendly packaging. The packaging for her products is supplied by Potek Packaging Ltd, which specialises in eco-friendly, recyclable packaging. In September, Tracey ordered fifty boxes of various types of eco-friendly packaging from Jim. She explained to Jim at the time that she was getting ready for the substantial number of orders she would usually get around Christmas time. The packaging is due to be delivered by 10· October. However, on 10th October Jim phones to say that he won't be able to deliver the packaging. Jim offers to supply Tracey with standard packaging, but Tracey refuses as she is worried about the reputational harm that would be caused to her brand if she used non-eco-friendly packaging. She cannot get eco-friendly packaging from any other source, because it is too late in the season. She has to cancel some orders that have already been placed on her website, and take most of her products off her website, meaning she misses out on Christmas orders and approximately €20,000 in profits that she would normally make at this time of year. In addition, she cannot make use of other ingredients she has purchased, worth €10,000, and she has wasted advertising costs of €2,000. Tracey wants damages to cover her lost profits and her wasted expenditure. Jim admits that Potek Packaging Ltd are in breach of contract, but refuses to pay damages to Tracey. He argues that Tracey could have mitigated her losses by accepting their offer to supply other packaging. He also argues that as most of her Christmas orders from customers had not been made yet, that she cannot definitively state that she would have made profits of €20,000, and cannot claim damages. Advise Tracey.

questions.description: Ross runs a transport company. In June, Marie, who runs a chain of supermarkets across Ireland, sends the following emai…
questions.text: Ross runs a transport company. In June, Marie, who runs a chain of supermarkets across Ireland, sends the following email to Ross: "We need frozen goods transported from Dublin Port to Cork - one delivery a week from 15 June to end of September. Let us know your rate and details." Ross replies: "That's great - our rate is €250 per delivery, based on a weekly delivery over that time period. Payment due monthly." Marie replies: "Fantastic - it's a deal!" However, the next week Ross decides that this was not in fact a profitable deal for him, and he would be better off entering a transport agreement with someone else. He calls Marie to cancel the agreement. Marie is furious, as she says they had a binding contract and it will now be more expensive to hire another transporter. Ross thinks he can escape the deal as Marie has not yet paid any money or any deposit. Ross also has an ongoing arrangement with a kitchen unit manufacturer, Kitchens Ltd, to transport kitchen units and kitchen equipment to different locations around the country. Whenever Kitchens Ltd needs something transported, the manager Carla simply phones Ross with the details and Ross organises the transport. This has happened approximately twice a month for the past two years, although there is no written contract in place. However, Carla has recently told Ross that she is not going to continue placing orders with him. Ross believes that an implied contract exists because of their ongoing relationship, and that Carla cannot simply cancel this contract. He believes that this implied contract includes an implied duty of good faith that requires Kitchens Ltd/Carla to give reasonable notice of six months to terminate the contract. Advise Ross.

questions.description: Tom is the manager of Mega Computers Ltd, which sells computers to both businesses and consumers. Tom was recently conta…
questions.text: Tom is the manager of Mega Computers Ltd, which sells computers to both businesses and consumers. Tom was recently contacted by Sally, the manager of Eyre Ltd, an engineering company. Sally wanted to purchase 30 computers for the business, and asked Tom to select computers which would be suitable to run complex engineering software. Tom recommended the "Bell 2020" model, and assured Sally that this model would be suitable to run complex engineering software. Sally signs a written sales contract for the purchase of 30 Bell 2020 computers. The contract makes no mention of the ability of the computer to run the software. Unfortunately, when the employees at Eyre Ltd begin to use their computers, it transpires that the computers are very slow when running the engineering software, causing frustration amongst the employees. In addition, 3 out of the 10 computers have blurry screens and cannot be used at all. Sally is furious and wishes to return the computers and get her money back. However, when she complains to Tom, he points to Term 6 of the Terms and Conditions on the back of the signed contract which states: "All sales are final and all statutory warranties are excluded. The seller is not liable for any loss caused by any defect in any product whatsoever. No returns." Tom wants to know if Sally has a cause of action and if Term 6 will be effective to prevent any such cause of action. Advise Tom.

questions.description: Sinéad owns a hairdressing salon. She decides to expand her business and in early January she hires Pat to build a fully…
questions.text: Sinéad owns a hairdressing salon. She decides to expand her business and in early January she hires Pat to build a fully completed extension on to the salon. The contract between Sinéad and Pat clearly states that the work is to be finished by June 30th.    Pat enters into a sub-contract with Charles, a local plumber in town. Charles agrees to carry out all the plumbing work on the extension by mid-May for €5,000. Pat emphasises that it is essential that this work be done on time. At the start of May Charles contacts Pat to say that the work is taking longer than he had anticipated as it is a "bigger job" than he thought. Charles says he is going to need an extra payment of €2,000 to enable him to hire extra workers so he can finish the job on time. Pat reluctantly agrees to pay the money and Charles manages to finish on time. However, once the work was done Pat writes Charles a letter demanding the return of the additional €2,000 paid, saying that it was not part of the original agreed price and that Charles has "done nothing extra for the cash". In addition, Sinéad is unhappy with the quality of some of Charles' plumbing work. She claims that the water pressure is too low for washing clients' hair and that there is a major leak from one of the sinks. She is threatening to sue Charles for breach of contract. Advise Charles on his legal position vis à vis (a) Pat and (b) Sinéad.

questions.description: Answer (a) or (b) Discuss the impact of the Unfair Terms in Consumer Contracts Directive (93/13/EEC) on Irish contract l…
questions.text: Answer (a) or (b) Discuss the impact of the Unfair Terms in Consumer Contracts Directive (93/13/EEC) on Irish contract law. In your view, has the Directive been positive for consumers? Or Discuss the approach of the Irish courts to a plea that a contract was entered into because of a common mistake of the contracting parties. In your view, should equitable relief be available for common mistake?

questions.description: Rebecca is a member of an online forum for fans of graphic novels and comics. She decides to sell some of her comics, so…
questions.text: Rebecca is a member of an online forum for fans of graphic novels and comics. She decides to sell some of her comics, so on Monday morning she places the following post on the forum: "Limited Time Offers: Early Edition Limited Copy of "Spiderman" for €50 and a copy of "Deadpool" signed by Declan Shalvey for €60. If interested email Rebecca at comicgeek@email.ie by noon Thursday." Cormac sees the post and decides that the Spiderman comic would be a good addition to his comic book collection. He immediately sends an email to Rebecca to say he is interested in buying the comic, but he accidentally says he will pay €500 for the comic. Rebecca immediately replies saying "Yes it's a deal!" Rebecca believes she has a contract with Cormac, which he wants to enforce, but Cormac refuses to pay, saying he clearly made a mistake and never intended to offer €500. Tom also sees Rebecca's post. Tom is a big fan of the Marvel artist Declan Shalvey, so he buys the signed Deadpool comic. However, a few months later he is at a comics event in Dublin when he bumps into Declan Shalvey, who says that it is not his signature on the comic and it must be a fake. Tom has contacted Rebecca to get his money back, claiming that she misrepresented that it was a signed copy when it is not. Rebecca says she bought the comic as signed, and did not know it was a fake either. Advise Rebecca

questions.description: IreTV is an Irish television producer, which makes TV shows and series and sells them on to RTE and other broadcasters. …
questions.text: IreTV is an Irish television producer, which makes TV shows and series and sells them on to RTE and other broadcasters. In July, IreTV hired Sinead, a very well-known and popular Irish actress, to play the lead role in a period drama Uptown Abbey. The contract specified that Sinead would have to be available for filming Mondays to Fridays from 1st September to 20th December, and a clause in the contract stated that she was also to take part in "promotion of the series Uptown Abbey through interviews and social media". In advance of recording, IreTV spent €500,000 on hiring and preparing a location for the series, as well as hiring personnel such as camera crews, engineers and costumer designers. However, after recording for the first 4 weeks Sinead failed to turn up for 4 mornings in a row with no explanation, refusing to answer her phone. She then announced n social media that once she's "finished filming this boring series about rich dead people" she's going to focus on her music career. The managers in IreTV are furious. They view this action as a breach of contract, giving them the right to terminate her contract and seek damages for wasted expenditure and /or loss of profits. Advise IreTV.

questions.description: John goes shopping in Dublin city centre every Saturday an4 regularly parks his car in the 'O'Connell Street car park in…
questions.text: John goes shopping in Dublin city centre every Saturday an4 regularly parks his car in the 'O'Connell Street car park in the centre of Dublin. There is a sign at the entrance that states "If you enter this Car park you agree to the Terms and Conditions of Parking, available at parksafe.ie/terms and conditions" John has noticed the sign on his way in, but has never checked the website to read the terms and conditions. One Saturday John parks his car in the O'Connell Street car park and pays for his ticket as normal, but when he returns to his car a large light falls from the ceiling of the car park onto his car. John sustains cuts and bruises, and the windscreen of his car is smashed. It transpires that the light fell as a result of the negligence of the car park staff. However, Carla, the manager of the car park, claims that John cannot bring an action against the owners of the car park in either contract or tort for his injuries or the damage to his car because of clause 12 of the "Terms and Conditions of Parking". Clause 12 excludes liability "in contract or tort or any other action" for "any damage to property or persons howsoever caused" while cars are left in the car park. Advise John on whether clause 12 has been incorporated into his contract, and, assuming it is incorporated, whether it can prevent him from bringing an action against the owners of the car park. Refer to consumer protection legislation in your answer.

questions.description: Ciara is the owner of a housing development in Donegal She has contracted with Liam to build 50 new houses. The contract…
questions.text: Ciara is the owner of a housing development in Donegal She has contracted with Liam to build 50 new houses. The contract between Ciara and Liam clearly states that the work is to be finished by 30th June 2020, and staged payments amounting to €5 million are to be paid to Liam. However, in March 2020 the government announces a government ban on all construction work due to the Covid-19 virus and Liam is forced to stop all work on the extension. When the ban is lifted and work recommences in mid- May it becomes clear that Liam will not be able to complete the work on time and will incur significant additional expenses in completing the work under new health and safety requirements, meaning he will suffer a loss. Liam wishes to either escape the contract entirely without liability, or to demand that Ciara pay him a higher sum of money for the work to reflect the new circumstances. Advise Liam on both of these options.
