questions.description: Sophie is a property developer who is interested in purchasing and developing a piece of land which is owned by Jack. Sh…
questions.text: Sophie is a property developer who is interested in purchasing and developing a piece of land which is owned by Jack. She approaches Jack one day and after an hour of discussions they agree that Jack will sell it to Sophie for €70,000. When Sophie asks if Jack would want a deposit, Jack says that they can "leave that sort of thing for the solicitors to sort out later". Later that evening, Sophie emails Jack the following: "Great to talk today! Am happy with the €70,000 price tag for the land. I'll get in touch with my solicitor tomorrow and get them to figure out all the formal stuff with your solicitor. Regards, Sophie" Jack immediately replies, writing: "Yes - fantastic - will chat then. Jack" However, the next day Jack contacts Sophie to say that he is not going to go ahead with the sale. He says that there was never a fully concluded agreement as they never agreed on a deposit and even if  there  was  an  agreement  it  was  only  an  oral  agreement  and not enforceable.. Sophie sends a cheque for €70,000 to Jack in an attempt to secure the sale, but Jack does not cash it, and returns the cheque. Sophie wishes to enforce the contract. Advise Sophie.

questions.description: Oisin is the manager of a transport company, with a fleet of 50 lorries and vans. In 2014, Oisin entered into a ten year…
questions.text: Oisin is the manager of a transport company, with a fleet of 50 lorries and vans. In 2014, Oisin entered into a ten year transport contract with Supermarket Ltd, a major Irish supermarket chain. Oisin agreed to transport and deliver goods from Supermarket Ltd's warehouses to its supermarkets for a period of ten years, at a fixed price of €1 million per year. However, by 2016, political crises and unexpected changes in market conditions have caused the price of diesel to increase by 20%. By 2019 diesel prices in Ireland have increased to such an extent that Oisin is performing the contract with Supermarket Ltd at a loss. Oisin wants to know if he has any grounds on which he can terminate the transport contract with Supermarket Ltd. In addition, in September 2019 Oisin entered into a written contract with a small retail company, Shop Ltd, whereby Oisin agreed to complete all its Christmas deliveries in November and December 2019, for a set fee, payable on 1 November. However, in October 2019, Shop Ltd decided to hire a different transport company which offered a cheaper rate, and they contacted Oisin to cancel the contract. Oisin felt like he had no choice to accept the cancellation, but now wants to sue for breach of contract. Shop Ltd have stated that they were entitled to cancel the contract because they had not paid a deposit and performance had not yet begun. Advise Oisin.

questions.description: Niamh owns a commercial cleaning business. She has recently entered into 3 new cleaning contracts, due to start in April…
questions.text: Niamh owns a commercial cleaning business. She has recently entered into 3 new cleaning contracts, due to start in April, and to facilitate this she orders new specialized cleaning equipment from Ross. The contract with Ross states that this equipment will be delivered by the end of March, but Ross fails to deliver at this date. Despite numerous emails and complaints, Ross does not deliver the equipment until mid-May. Niamh is furious. The delay in getting this equipment has meant that she has had to delay the start date for 2 of her contracts, and her third client has cancelled the contract altogether. This third contract was a particularly lucrative contract with a pharmaceutical company, which would have earned her substantially more than her regular contracts. Niamh seeks damages to compensate for her financial losses and for the emotional stress the delay has caused her. Ross admits that he is in breach of contract, but refuses to pay damages to Niamh. He says that Niamh could have mitigated her losses by hiring equipment when there was a delay in delivery, and that he did not know about the contract with the pharmaceutical company as Niamh never told him about it. He also says that it's "not his problem" if the delay caused Niamh stress. Advise Niamh

questions.description: Maire is the owner of the Comfy Cafe. She decides to expand her cafe and hires Bob to build a fully completed extension …
questions.text: Maire is the owner of the Comfy Cafe. She decides to expand her cafe and hires Bob to build a fully completed extension on to the existing premises. The contract between Maire and Bob clearly states that the work is to be finished by 31 January 2020, and it includes a penalty clause which says that Bob is to be liable for the loss caused by any delays, howsoever caused. Bob enters into a sub-contract with Charles, a local plumber in town. Charles agrees to carry out all the plumbing work on the extension by 31 December 2019. Bob emphasises that it is essential that this work be done on time. However, by mid-November 2019 it becomes clear that Charles will not be able to complete the work on time. Charles demands €5,000 to enable him to hire extra workers so he can finish the job on time. Bob reluctantly agrees to this and Charles manages to finish on time. However, once the work is done Bob writes Charles a letter saying that he is not going to pay Charles the extra €5,000. He says he only agreed to the extra money under duress, and that Charles "did nothing extra to earn the cash".  In addition Maire is unhappy with some of the plumbing work completed by Charles. She claims that water leaks from the pipes and is causing damage to her floors. She is threatening to sue Charles for breach of contract. Advise Charles.

questions.description: Ben owns a business which produces organic fruit juices and drinks. He emails Joe, a local farmer, offering to buy 100 c…
questions.text: Ben owns a business which produces organic fruit juices and drinks. He emails Joe, a local farmer, offering to buy 100 crates of organic strawberries for supply the following month, to be delivered in four weekly instalments. Joe replies, saying "Yes, that should be no problem. Just fill out and return the order form attached to this email and we will deliver." Ben fills out the order form, typing his name at the bottom of the first page of the order form where it says "signature", and emails it back to Joe. The first page of the order form says "All orders are subject to the terms and conditions of sale". These terms and conditions of sale are on the second page of the order form, but Ben does not read them. One term states as follows: "In the event of any fruit sold not complying with the express terms of the contract of sale or any statutory warranties, we will refund all payments made to us by the buyer and this shall be the limit of our obligation. We hereby exclude all liability for any direct or indirect loss or damage arising from the use of any fruit supplied by us and for any consequential loss or damage arising out of such use." These terms and conditions are also contained in the delivery docket which Ben signs when the fruit is delivered each week. However, in the fourth week Joe inadvertently sprays his fruit crop with chemical pesticides. When Ben uses this fruit a week's production is contaminated and cannot be sold as "organic" juice. When Ben sues Joe for breach of contract, Joe seeks to rely on the limitation clause. Ben argues that the limitation clause is not incorporated in the contract or alternatively that it is not effective to protect Joe. Advise Ben.

questions.description: In 2015 Pub Enterprises Ltd (PE Ltd) entered into a 10 year lease with Max, the owner of a large premises, which PE Ltd …
questions.text: In 2015 Pub Enterprises Ltd (PE Ltd) entered into a 10 year lease with Max, the owner of a large premises, which PE Ltd planned to use as a pub and nightclub. However, in January 2018 owing to construction works causing disruption in the area, PE Ltd was struggling to pay the agreed rent. Max agreed to accept a reduced rent from PE Ltd, "for as long as construction works continue". There was no consideration for the promise to accept a lower rent, nor was the promise formalised in a deed under seal. By June 2019 the construction works were finished and PE Ltd began paying the higher rate of rent again. However, Max is now claiming payment of the unpaid portion of the original rate of rent for the period from January 2018 - May 2019. In addition, since 2015 PE Ltd has regularly ordered champagne from Bubbly Ltd. They have no written supply contract. Each time PE Ltd needs a delivery they simply contact Bubbly Ltd and order what they need. This occurs approximately once a month. However, PE Ltd decide that they want to focus more on craft beers, and mention to Bubble Ltd that they will not be placing further orders . Bubbly Ltd however claims that PE Ltd cannot simply terminate their "arrangement" without notice, and that there is an implied term that at least six months' notice should be given of any intention to terminate the contract. PE Ltd claims there is no such contract or implied term. Advise PE Ltd.

questions.description: Aoife owns an outdoor forest adventure centre for children. She advertises the sale of the business and premises in the …
questions.text: Aoife owns an outdoor forest adventure centre for children. She advertises the sale of the business and premises in the local newspaper. Sam is looking to open up a small business in the local area and, seeing the advertisement, he visits the premises. Aoife tells him that the profits of the business are usually about €30,000 per year. She offers Sam the opportunity to look at the business accounts, but Sam says he will take her word for it because he is in a hurry. The next day, Aoife's insurance company contacts her to say that owing to increased claims in the sector that they will be increasing the insurance premiums for all adventure centres by 40%. Aoife decides not to tell Sam as she does not want to risk him deciding to not buy the business. The following week Sam enters into a written contract to purchase the business and premises for €200,000. The contract makes no mention of the profit figures or the availability of insurance. A week after completion of the sale, Sam discovers that profits had not exceeded €20,000 in the last three years. In addition, when he attempts to get insurance he finds that the premiums are so excessive that the profits are likely to be greatly reduced this year. As a result Sam wishes he had never bought the premises and business. Advise Sam on whether he has any cause of action against Aoife and on the remedies that may be available to him.

questions.description: Jack is a 70 year old farmer who lives on his own in a rural area in the west of Ireland. He is in ill health and quite …
questions.text: Jack is a 70 year old farmer who lives on his own in a rural area in the west of Ireland. He is in ill health and quite dependent on his neighbour, Sheila, who regularly visits him and assists him with basic household jobs such as cooking and cleaning. One day Sheila approaches Jack and asks him to sign a petition to stop the closure of a local school. Jack doesn't have his reading glasses and so he signs the petition without reading it. Jack's nephew Calm visits Jack later that year and upon investigation discovers that the "petition" is, in fact, a bank guarantee form which guarantees Sheila's debts up to €25,000. Calm is concerned that his uncle may become liable under this guarantee and wants to know if there is any way the guarantee may be void. When Calm is visiting his uncle he attends a local car auction. He bids €5,000 for a car which is advertised as "without reserve". However, this is the only bid and the car is withdrawn from the auction by Audrey, on the instructions of the owner of the car. That evening Calm sees the car for sale on a car sales website for €9,000.  Colm wishes to know if he has a binding agreement to buy the car for €5,000. Advise Calm.

questions.description: Carol is a landscape gardener, who specialises in designing, building and maintaining gardens and outdoor areas for busi…
questions.text:
Carol is a landscape gardener, who specialises in designing, building and maintaining gardens and outdoor areas for businesses such as hotels. Carol receives a phone call from a local mushroom grower, Fred, asking if Carol would be interested in taking a truck full of mushroom compost for use in her business, as otherwise he "would just be throwing it out". Carol is delighted and immediately agrees to take the compost. When she collects the compost the next day Carol is so happy with it that she promises she will pay him €200. Fred is delighted by this; however, Carol never pays him the money. Fred is now threatening to sue Carol for the money. In addition, Carol decides that she needs to buy a small digger for her business. On Monday, she emails Niamh, the owner of "Garden Equipment Ltd", to enquire about the possibility of buying a "Micro-Digger". That afternoon, Niamh responds as follows: "Thank you for your enquiry. We have one Micro-Digger for sale, at a cost of €10,000 including VAT and delivery charges. We can keep it for you until the weekend, so please let us know by then if you wish to purchase." On Friday Carol replies, saying "That's great, I'll take it." However, Niamh replies and says that she sold the Micro-Digger to somebody else on Wednesday and so she cannot sell it to Carol. Carol is angry because she cannot find the digger for sale at this price anywhere else, 
and she believes she has a binding contract with Niamh. Advise Carol.

questions.description: John goes to his local cinema approximately once a month. He usually pays for his ticket in advance on the cinema websit…
questions.text: John goes to his local cinema approximately once a month. He usually pays for his ticket in advance on the cinema website, and collects the ticket from a machine in the cinema foyer. When he purchases the ticket on the website, he always ticks a box that says he has "read and agreed to the terms and conditions of sale". These are available through a hyperlink to another part of the cinema website, but he has never in fact read these terms. Last Saturday, John bought his ticket online as per usual. However, when he was in the cinema he tripped on an electric cable which had been left in the cinema aisle by a member of staff. He badly injured his back, and wants to bring an action against the cinema for negligence. However, Carla, the manager of the cinema, claims that John cannot bring an action against the cinema in either contract or tort because of clause 12 of the Terms and Conditions of Sale, as available on the website. Clause 12 states: "The cinema is not liable in contract, tort or any other action for any damage to property or persons howsoever caused while on the premises." Advise John on whether clause 12 has been incorporated into his contract, and, assuming it is incorporated, whether it can prevent him from bringing an action against the cinema. Refer to any applicable consumer legislation in your answer. Please note there is no need to discuss the law of negligence in your answer.

questions.description: In March 2018, Peter was contacted by Elaine, an employee of Invest Ltd, about an investment opportunity being offered t…
questions.text: In March 2018, Peter was contacted by Elaine, an employee of Invest Ltd, about an investment opportunity being offered to clients of her firm. Elaine sent Peter a 7 page brochure regarding a property investment fund which was seeking investors for a shopping centre in Cork. This brochure stated that the redeveloped shopping centre would provide 120,000 square metres of retail space, and that planning permission was available for a hotel on the land. In April 2018 Peter agreed to invest €1 million in the property fund. The formal contractual documentation made no mention of the amount of retail space or the planning permission for a hotel. However, in May 2018 Peter was told that in fact only 90,000 square meters of retail space would be available. In addition, it transpired that although planning permission had been available in March 2018, it had expired at the start of April 2018 and further applications would be necessary to build a hotel. Peter regrets investing in the shopping centre and wishes to get his money back. However, when he contacts Elaine to complain she tells him that he "should have done his due diligence and checked the facts and figures before he invested". Advise Peter.

questions.description: Coldstore Ltd is a transporter of frozen food and pharmaceuticals to hotels and businesses across Ireland, with its own …
questions.text:
Coldstore Ltd is a transporter of frozen food and pharmaceuticals to hotels and businesses across Ireland, with its own fleet of refrigerated trucks to make deliveries. In April 2018, Coldstore ltd entered into a contract with Bliss Hotel, where it agreed to deliver a week's supply of frozen and refrigerated food to the hotel every Monday for a period of one year. However, one week in May 2018, due to a fault in the truck's refrigeration system, the food was considered unsafe for consumption on arrival at the hotel and had to be thrown out. As a result, the hotel restaurant had to close for a week. 
Coldstore Ltd accepted that it was in breach of contract, and agreed to refund the price of the wasted food. However, Bliss Hotels is claiming damages for the lost profits from the closure of the restaurant. Coldstore ltd believes that it is not responsible for this loss, and that Bliss Hotel could have mitigated its loss by purchasing in other sources of food. In May 2018, Coldstore Ltd also agreed to be a transporter for a large pharmaceutical company, Xyntath Ltd. Coldstore Ltd's manager Tom signed the pharmaceutical company's standard form contract. In June 2018, one of the deliveries was two days late due to a shortage of drivers. Xyntath Ltd is claiming damages and is seeking to rely on the following clause of its contract with Coldstore Ltd: "In the event that any delivery is more than 24 hours late, the transporter shall be responsible for the costs of all and any delays in production and a payment of 120% of the cost of the material transported shall be immediately due." Coldstore Ltd believes that this is a penalty clause and should not be enforceable. Advise Coldstore Ltd.

questions.description: Paula wants to buy a new Playstation. She finds one on the shelf of a local computer game shop, GamesRUs at a price of €…
questions.text: Paula wants to buy a new Playstation. She finds one on the shelf of a local computer game shop, GamesRUs at a price of €350. However, when she brings it to the counter to pay for it the sales assistant refuses to sell it to her, saying that there was a mistake on the price tag and it should say €450. Paula thinks she has a contractual right to Playstation for €350 and wants to know if the sales assistant was entitled to refuse to sell. Paula decides to try to purchase a Playstation online instead. She is shocked when she seeks that the current version is on sale on the Arghus website for €4.50. She decides to act quickly and immediately places an order for 5 Playstations, thinking that she can keep one and sell the others. She receives an automated email confirmation stating her order has been accepted. However the next day she receives an email from Arghus stating that there had been an error on the website and it should have said €450. Arghus states that Paula should have realized this was a mistake and is refusing to go ahead with the sale. Advise Paula.

questions.description: Formula News (FN) is a popular newspaper in Ireland, with over half a million regular readers. In July 2018 FN opened a …
questions.text: Formula News (FN) is a popular newspaper in Ireland, with over half a million regular readers. In July 2018 FN opened a tender competition to four interested parties, inviting them to submit their best offer in a sealed bid for sponsorship of the crossword puzzle in the newspaper for a period of three years. The tender email stated that the contract would be awarded to the highest bidder. Max, the owner of a courier company, submitted a tender offering 5% above the highest priced bid received. However FN accepted a bid from Tina, the owner of a chain of stationery shops, for a total sum of €1.5 million. Max considers himself to be the highest bidder and is seeking to enforce the contract. Each month for the last 5 years, FN has placed a large order for printing paper with Rollout Ltd. There is no express written contract between them but the Manager of Tollout Ltd, Carrie, has said on several occasions that she is “delighted with the arrangement” and that she “looks forward to developing the relationship in future years”. FN recently discovered that it can obtain paper at a lower price from another company and emailed Carrie to say that they would no longer be ordering from Rollout Ltd. Carrie claims that this is a breach of FN’s “duty to act in good faith: and is threatening to sue for breach of contract. Advise Formula News (FN)

questions.description: There is great excitement in Dublin city when it is announced in January 2018 that there will be a visit by Francis Mari…
questions.text: There is great excitement in Dublin city when it is announced in January 2018 that there will be a visit by Francis Mario, a popular but controversial singer, in August 2018. The plans for the visit include a procession through the city centre. As soon as the visit is announced, groups who object to the visit by Francis Mario state that they will have a counter- protest on the same day as the procession. Tom plans to travel from Galway to Dublin to view the procession. In July 2018 he goes to the website of the Central Hotel and pays €500 for a hotel room in the Central Hotel for the Saturday night of the Procession weekend. This is five times the normal price of a room in Central Hotel, and the hotel’s website describes it as a “Francis Mario Special Rate”. When Tom books the hotel he ticks a box that states “I agree to the terms and conditions as stated on the Central Hotel website”, but does not read the terms. Term 16, which is available on the website, states: “This is a non- refundable payment. Central Hotel is not liable to repay money paid in the event of cancellation by either party for the booked dates, for any reason whatsoever.” On 1st August, the visit by Francis Mario is cancelled. His official website state that this is because of an illness, but there are rumours that is it is because of the protests which were planned for the same day. Tom contacts the Central Hotel to cancel his booking, but is told that he is not entitled to the return of the €500. Tom believes that the contract is frustrated and wishes to recover this money. Advise Tom. Can Tom invoke any consumer protection legislation?

questions.description: In May 2017, the National Swimming Centre (NSC) contracted with Bodgeit Ltd for the construction of a new diving pool. T…
questions.text: In May 2017, the National Swimming Centre (NSC) contracted with Bodgeit Ltd for the construction of a new diving pool. The contract price was €2 million, and the NSC provided detailed specifications for the precise depth, design and materials for use in the build. The works were completed in July 2018, but the NSC is unhappy with the work. The contract states that the diving pool should be 5.2 metres deep, but it is in fact only 4.8m deep. This means that the diving pool as the Olympics for divers who wish to complete in international competitions such as the Olympics. The NSC wants Bodgeit Ltd to pay damages of €2.5 million so the pool can be dismantled and rebuilt according to the specifications given. However, Bodgeit Ltd states that the diving pool is still useful for most diving, and has only a slightly reduced value. They refuse to pay the level of damages sought by the NSC but offer to pay €40, 000 to reflect the reduced value of the pool. In addition, in June 2018 the NSC entered into a 1 year written contract with an advertising company, Publicity Ltd. Publicity Ltd agrees to pay the NSC €550, 000 in return for the right to place advertisements around the complex from August 2018 to July 2019. The fee is to be paid in full in August 2018. However in July 2018 Publicity Ltd contacts the NSC to cancel the contract, saying that it is entitled to do so as neither party has performed their side of the contract. The NSC wants to know if Publicity Ltd is liable for breach of contract and what damages might be payable. Advise the National Swimming Centre (NSC)

questions.description: Paula is starting up a restaurant and needs to buy a special extra-large oven for the kitchen. On Monday she emails Niam…
questions.text: Paula is starting up a restaurant and needs to buy a special extra-large oven for the kitchen. On Monday she emails Niamh, the owner of "Ovens Ltd", to enquire about the possibility of buying an "XL Super Oven". That afternoon, Niamh responds as follows: "Thank you for your enquiry. We have one XL Super Oven for sale, at a cost of €10,000 including VAT and delivery charges. We can keep it for you until the weekend, so please let us know by then if you wish to purchase." On Friday Paula replies, saying "That's great, I'll take it." However, Niamh replies and says that she sold the XL Super Oven to somebody else on Wednesday and so she cannot sell it to Paula. Paula is angry because she cannot find the oven for sale at this price anywhere else, and she believes she has a binding contract with Niamh. A month before her restaurant is due to open Paula realises that she needs additional financial support if the business is to succeed. She enters into an agreement with a local food supplier, Brian, who agrees to invest €20,000 in the business in return for a promise that Paula will enter into a contract with him for the supply of all foodstuffs to the business for the next year. However, after two weeks of discussions they fail to agree on important terms of the supply contract, including prices and delivery and payment arrangements. Paula wants to end negotiations, but Brian is insisting that they have an "agreement to agree" and she is under a contractual duty to negotiate the contract.

questions.description: Jack makes organic homemade jams which he sells at outdoor markets and fairs. In January 2017, Jack entered into a signe…
questions.text: Jack makes organic homemade jams which he sells at outdoor markets and fairs. In January 2017, Jack entered into a signed contract with his friend Sarah, the organiser of "Gourmet Market", a major outdoor market held weekly in a square in Dublin city centre. This agreement stated that Jack could hire a stall at the "Gourmet Market" at a rate of €400 a month for a period of two years. However, after the contract was signed, Sarah said to Jack over the phone that as they were friends he could simply pay €300 a month and "not worry about the legal paperwork". From January 2017 until August 2017, Jack paid €300 a month to Sarah for his stall. He also turned down an opportunity to hire a stall at the rival "Greedy Market" for €350 a month on the basis that he had a better deal with Sarah at "Gourmet Market". However, in September 2017, Sarah told Jack that she was putting the rent back up to €400 a month. Jack wants to know if he has a contractual right to continue to hire the stall for €300 a month. In January 2017, Jack also entered into an individual agreement with Laura, the organiser of "Shoots Market", a small outdoor market held weekly in Galway. This agreement stated that Jack could hire a stall at "Shoots market" for €250 a month for a period of two years, and that Laura would "use her best endeavours to promote Shoots market". However, the market does not attract many customers in the summer of 2017. Jack feels that this is because Laura has not placed sufficient advertisements in local newspapers or magazines, and has not made use of online resources to advertise to tourists. Jack has seen a significant reduction in profits and believes this is because Laura is failing to.uphold her part of the bargain. However, Laura says that the phrase "best endeavours" is too vague to be enforceable, and that other factors such as poor weather have affected the number of customers attending the market. Advise Jack on his position vis-å-vis Sarah and Laura.

questions.description: Carla runs a small business in which she takes groups of tourists out to a small island off the coast of Donegal. She bu…
questions.text: Carla runs a small business in which she takes groups of tourists out to a small island off the coast of Donegal. She buys a fishing boat from Sean, and during the course of negotiations she mentions that she will be using the boat to take tourists out on trips to the island. However, a month after buying the boat, the engine malfunctions and starts to emit fumes and black smoke. The fumes are so bad that she has to cancel several trips which had been booked. Carla wants a refund and damages to compensate for the tours which were cancelled. However, when she contacts Sean, he refuses to pay her anything, and says "sure you shouldn't have been using a fishing boat for tourists anyway". He also points to the following exclusion clause in the signed contract of sale: "Term 7: "All sales are final. The buyer herby waives any implied statutory warranty of merchantability or of fitness for a particular purpose. We hereby exclude all liability for any consequential loss or damage caused as a result of any defect." In addition, Sean says that even if he is in breach of contract, that Carla failed to mitigate her losses, and she should have hired out a different boat rather than cancel her trips to the island. Advise Carla.

questions.description: Matilda owns a "play cafe " — a coffee shop with a play area for children. The play cafe is located on the High Street i…
questions.text:
Matilda owns a "play cafe " — a coffee shop with a play area for children. The play cafe is located on the High Street in the centre of Galway, next door to a car park, and is popular with parents in the area. She advertises the sale of the business and premises in the local newspaper. Tony is looking to open up a small business in the local area, and seeing the advertisement, he visits the premises. Matilda tells him that the profits of the business are usually about €20,000 per year and that she anticipates that profits will increase by at least 
15% in the next year because of the increasing interest of parents in meeting up in child- friendly venues. She offers Tony the opportunity to look at the business accounts, but Tony says he will take her word for it because he is in a hurry. The day after Tony's visit, the local authority notifies Matilda that the High Street will soon become a traffic free zone and the car park next to the play cafe will be closed. Matilda decides not to pass this information to Tony because she knows that her customers rely on being able to park close to the play cafe. The following week Tony enters into a written contract to purchase the business and premises for €100,000. The contract makes no mention of the profit figures or the proposed closing of the car park. A month after completion of the sale, Tony asks his accountant to read the accounts and discovers that they show that profits had not exceeded €15,000 in the last three years. That week, the High Street becomes a traffic free zone, causing a drastic reduction in trade as parents can no longer park nearby. As a result Tony wishes he had never bought the play cafe. Advise Tony on whether he has any cause of action against Matilda and on the remedies that may be available to him.

questions.description: Ross is the manager of a construction site. He needs to hire a large crane for the site. On Monday he emails Carla, the …
questions.text: Ross is the manager of a construction site. He needs to hire a large crane for the site. On Monday he emails Carla, the owner of ‘Crane Hire Ltd’ to enquire about the price of hiring an ‘XL Super Crane’ for 6 months. That afternoon Carla responds as follows: “Thank you for your enquiry. We have one XL Super Crane in stock, at the price of €2,000 per month plus delivery charges.” On Friday Ross replies, saying “That’s great, I’ll take it! Can you deliver next week?” However Carla replies to tell him that she hired out the XL Super Crane to somebody else on Wednesday and so she cannot hire it to Ross. Ross is angry because he cannot find the crane for hire at this price anywhere else, and he believes he has a binding contract with Carla. Advise Ross. AND Paula sees the following advertisement in a magazine for a product called Miracle Teeth Whitener, produced by MTW Ltd: “Miracle Teeth Whitener: Make those Teeth Shine! Use our Miracle Teeth Whitener twice a day and see the difference! Guaranteed results after four weeks or MTW Ltd will pay you €500!” Excited by the advertisement, Paula buys a months supply of Miracle Teeth Whitener and takes it as directed. However, after four weeks it has had no effect. When she contacts MTW Ltd they refuse to pay her €500, saying that the advertisement was not a serious offer, and even if there was an offer, Paula did not accept it. Paula wants to know if she has a contract with MTW Ltd and whether she can claim her €500. Advise Paula. (Please focus your answer on Contract Law issues; there is no need to discuss possible consumer protection measures.)

questions.description: Carol is a landscape gardener, who specialises in designing, building and maintaining gardens and outdoor areas for busi…
questions.text: Carol is a landscape gardener, who specialises in designing, building and maintaining gardens and outdoor areas for businesses such as hotels. Carol receives a phone call from a local mushroom grower, Fred, asking if Carol would be interested in taking a truck full of mushroom compost for use in her business, as otherwise he “would just be throwing it out”. Carol is delighted and immediately agrees to take the compost. When she collects the compost the next day Carol is so happy with it that she promises she will pay him €200. Fred is delighted by this; however Carol never pays him the money. Fred wants to know if Carol’s promise to pay the money is binding. In June Carol designs an outdoor area for a local businessman, Pat, for an agreed price of €10,000. Pat is to pay Carol by cheque by July 15th. However, by the end of July Pat has still not paid Carol for her services. Carol presses for payment, to no avail. Eventually in August, Pat tells Carol that in the “current economic climate” he is not sure he will be able to pay her in full, but he offers her €6,000 in settlement of the debt. He tells her that if this offer is not accepted then nothing will be paid. Carol is worried about her cash flow, and reluctantly accepts Pat’s offer. Pat pays Carol €6,000 by cheque. A month later Carol changes her mind and looks for the balance, €4,000, from Pat. However, Pat says that they had a binding agreement that she would accept €6,000 and she cannot now claim the €4,000. Advise Carol on her legal position vis a vis (a) Fred and (b) Pat.

questions.description: Owen owns a business which manufactures bedroom furniture, such as wardrobes and beds. He enters into a contract with Ti…
questions.text: Owen owns a business which manufactures bedroom furniture, such as wardrobes and beds. He enters into a contract with Timber Supplies Ltd (TS Ltd) for the purchase of 10 crates of timber at a cost of €5,000. Clause 8 of the signed written contract states: “All sales are final. The buyer hereby waives any implied statutory warranty of merchantability or of fitness for a particular purpose. We hereby exclude all liability for any consequential loss or damage arising from the use of any timber supplied by us.” Owen uses the timber to make furniture, complaining that the timber has very quickly become warped as is of inferior quality. He has to refund his customers, suffering a loss of €20,000 as a result. When Owen sues TS Ltd for breach of contract, TS Ltd seeks to rely Clause 8 to exclude their liability. In addition, in April 2017 Owen enters into a 1 year written contract with an advertising company, Publicity Ltd. Publicity Ltd agrees to help design and maintain a new website for Owen, and to assist of the placement of advertisement on other websites. Work on the contract is due to begin in June 2017, with the website going online in July. A fee of €6,000 is agreed payable in two instalments in June 2017 and January 2018. However, in May 2017, Owen decides that he wanted to work with a different advertising company which offers a cheaper rate and he contacts Publicity Ltd to cancel the contract. Publicity Ltd accepts the cancellation but is now threatening to sue for breach of contract. Owen thinks he was entitled to cancel the contract because he had not paid a deposit or any instalment of the price due. Advise Owen on his legal position vis a vis (a)Timber Supplies Ltd (TS Ltd) and (b)Publicity Ltd

questions.description: Aoife, John and Max are members of a pop band, “High Notes”, which has recently had some success in the Irish charts. In…
questions.text: Aoife, John and Max are members of a pop band, “High Notes”, which has recently had some success in the Irish charts. In January 2017 the band is approached by Sheena, the producer of a popular brand of bottled water called “Highland Water”. Sheena is interested in entering into an endorsement contract with the band, under which the band would receive €75,000 in return for allowing their image to be used in an advertising campaign and on the packaging of the bottled water from April 2017 to March 2018. During the pre-contractual negotiations, Sheena asks the band whether they have plans to do a tour of concerts in the upcoming year. They all nod enthusiastically, and Aoife says that she is “looking forward to travelling across Ireland and elsewhere, playing music”. Before the contract is signed, Aoife, John and Max all take part in a promotional photoshoot for Sheena. However, one month after the contract is signed, Aoife leaves the band. It transpires that Aoife had been unhappy with the band’s focus on pop music, and wanted to pursue a career in traditional Irish music. John and Max decide that they will continue to sing as “High Notes”, but without Aoife the band is a flop, selling only a trivial amount of records and concert tickets. Meanwhile, Aoife is happy travelling across Ireland playing traditional music. Sheena is furious that Aoife has left the band. However, when she examines the contract in detail she realises that it was poorly drafted, and contains no term stating that the band must remain together in its current composition for the duration of the endorsement. She is seeking to escape the contract on the basis of misrepresentation, as she believes that the band misrepresented their position and their intentions during the pre-contractual negotiations, and failed to disclose the possibility that Aoife would leave the band to take up  traditional Irish music. She says if she had known this she would never have entered into the contact, However, Joh and Max say that if Sheena had looked at certain websites and blogs online, she would have seen rumours that Aoife wanted to leave, and it wasn’t the band’s fault that Sheena didn’t do her research. Advise Sheena.

questions.description: Kate wants to encourage her 18 year old niece Sarah to do well in her first year university law exams, so she tells her …
questions.text: Kate wants to encourage her 18 year old niece Sarah to do well in her first year university law exams, so she tells her that if “does lots of study and gets good grades” that she will pay her €1,000. Six months later Sarah contacts Kate to let her know that she passed all her first year law exams with mostly “C” grades and wants to claim her €1,000. Kate argues that she wasn’t serious when she offered Sarah the money and that it was only informal encouragement to a family member which is not legally binding. In addition she does not feel that passing her first year law exams with mostly “C” grades counts as getting “good grades” and that her promise was inherently uncertain. She wants to know if she has to pay Sarah the €1,000. Kate is worried that she might have to pay Sarah the money, so she decides to sell her car to make some money. On Monday she contacts Declan, a local car dealer, to discuss the possible purchase of her car. He promises to pay Kate €2,000 for the car, payment upon delivery. Kate is delighted and promises to deliver the car the following Friday. However, the next day Declan phones Kate to inform her that he has changed his mind as he has too many second hand cars to stock already. He claims that as she has not performed “her part of the deal” by delivering the car to him, he is entitled to do so. Kate wants to sue Declan for breach of contract. Advise Kate. Refer to cases in your answer.

questions.description: John is a musician and songwriter. In 2016 John wrote a song, the chorus of which he used a substantial excerpt from the…
questions.text: John is a musician and songwriter. In 2016 John wrote a song, the chorus of which he used a substantial excerpt from the song “Best Wishes” by Elaine De Burgh. John agreed to pay Elaine 50% of the royalties from his song. However, two months later in separate litigation it emerged that, due to a legal technicality Elaine had never has copyright in the song “Best Wishes”. Therefore John could have used the excerpt without obtaining her permission and without agreeing to pay her a portion of his royalties. John believes that they were both mistaken as to their positions in entering the contract. He wants to know if he can get out of the obligation to pay Elaine a portion of his royalties. John decides to buy a grand piano with some of the profits from his song. He attends a local auction hall at which vintage musical instruments are regularly sold. The auction brochure states that Lot 106 is a grand piano, up for auction “without reserve”. John bids €5,000 for the piano, but is outbid by another individual at the auction, who bids €6,000. This bid is accepted by the auctioneer, Sorcha. John subsequently discovers that the individual who outbid him is in fact the owner of the piano, Conor, who was not happy with the piano being sold for only €5,000. John wants to know if he has any contractual rights in this situation. Advise John. Refer to cases in your answer. (There is no need to discuss any copyright law in this question.)

questions.description: Niamh produces organic homemade face creams and sells them to individual consumers online, via her website, and to beaut…
questions.text: Niamh produces organic homemade face creams and sells them to individual consumers online, via her website, and to beauty salons across Ireland. Sharon orders a tub of Shea Butter Face Cream from Niamh’s website. However, when she uses it she suffers a skin rash. The Shea Butter Face Cream has caused no problems to any other people who have used it, and Sharon admits that she has particularly sensitive skin. Nonetheless, she is threatening to bring an action against Niamh for breach of contract on the basis that the cream is not of merchantable quality as required by the Sale of Goods Act 1893 (as amended). The terms of and conditions of sale on Niamh’s website do not contain any exclusion clause. Niamh wants to know if she could be in breach of contract. In addition, Niamh enters into a contract with a chain of beauty salons Cream Skin Ltd (S Ltd), who agrees to purchase 200 tubs of her Coconut Butter Face Cream at a cost of €5,000, for use in their salons. Clause 8 of the signed written sales contract between Niamh and CS Ltd states: “The buyer herby waives any implied statutory warrant of merchantability or of fitness for  a particular purpose. In the even that any cream sold is not of merchantable quality of fit for a particular purpose, the seller will refund all payments and this shall be the limit of the seller’s obligation. The seller herby excludes all liability for any loss or damage arising from the use of any face cream or other product supplied and for any consequential loss or damage arising out of such use.” Unfortunately, a significant number of tubs of Coconut Butter Face Cream have been contaminated and ten of CS Ltd’s clients suffer a severe facial rash as a result. CS Ltd is now claiming a complete refund of the €5,000 paid, plus damages of €25,000 from Niamh for breach of contract. Niamh seeks to rely on Clause 8 to limit her liability to the €5,000 paid for the face cream. Advise Niamh. Refer to cases in your answer.

questions.description: John is a farmer who wishes to obtain a loan of €500,000 from his bank to develop his family farm in Co. Leitrim. His wi…
questions.text: John is a farmer who wishes to obtain a loan of €500,000 from his bank to develop his family farm in Co. Leitrim. His wife Rose inherited a house in Dublin city from her aunt, and John tells Rose that he needs her to act as a guarantor for the loan and the charge her interest in the house as security for the loan. Rose agrees to this as she is worried about the future of the farm. When she is in the bank, she is asked to sign a form which states that she has received independent legal advice from a solicitor, but in fact she has not received any such legal advice. When she queries signing the form, the bank manager states “Oh it’s just a formality. We never enforce such things anyway. You can go talk to your solicitor about it if you’re worried though.” Rose signs, but does not consult her solicitor at any point. John fails to keep up with the loan repayments, and the bank is attempting to take possession of Rose’s house. Rose has since reviewed the loan and guarantee documentation, and noticed that some of the terms seem somewhat one sided in favour of the bank. For example, term 31 states: “The bank is not bound by any oral representation made by its agents, including bank managers and bank officials, prior to or after signing a guarantee.” Rose believes that this is unfair as she relied on the bank manager’s statement that the bank never enforces guarantees. She says she would not have signed the guarantee if he had not offered her this reassurance. The bank has conceded that John and Rose acted as consumers in this context. Advise Rose. Refer to cases in your answer.

questions.description: Discuss the approach of the Irish courts to penalty clauses in contracts. Refer to cases in your answer. Parties to a co…
questions.text: Discuss the approach of the Irish courts to penalty clauses in contracts. Refer to cases in your answer. Parties to a contract may include a clause which states that should one of them fail to meet their contractual obligations a fixed sum of money will be payable to the injured party as compensation. If this clause is designed to deter a party from breaching the contract by penalising him if he does then it will be deemed a ‘penalty clause’ and will be frowned upon by the courts. Dunlop Pneumatic Tyre v New Garage and Motor Co [1915] AC 79 The court held that a clause will be deemed a penalty clause if: It lists a sum that is “extravagant and unconscionable” when compared to the loss that could occur in respect of the breach of contract- Irish Telephone Rentals Ltd v Irish Civil Service Building Society Ltd [1991] ILRM 880- a clause that did not attempt in any consistent way to identify actual loss was held to be a penalty clause. The breach of contract is a failure to pay money and the damages for the breach exceed the sum to be paid under the contract- O’Donnell v Truck and Machinery Sales [1998] 4 IR 191- the Supreme Court held that a failure to pay a sum cannot result in damages that exceed the sum with interest. The sum of damages provided for in the clause is the same regardless of the damage caused by the breach of contract- Schiesse International v Gallagher (1971) 106 ILTR 22- a clause requiring an employee to repay his employer for training if he left their employment within three years was deemed to be a penalty clause as it applied equally regardless of the employee’s departure date.

questions.description: Crockery and clothes Ltd (CC Ltd) is a manufacturer of commemorative mugs and clothing and Sean is the owner of a sports…
questions.text: Crockery and clothes Ltd (CC Ltd) is a manufacturer of commemorative mugs and clothing and Sean is the owner of a sports shop in Dublin. In June 2016, Sean emailed CC Ltd’s sales manager, Tina with an enquiry for the manufacture of commemorative mugs to a specific design for the Olympic games in August 2016. Time replied, by email, that the design required would likely cost €3 per mug for the first 1,000 and €2 per mug for any ordered above 1000. Tina added that they would be very busy in the run up to the Olympic games and CC Ltd thus required orders to be placed a s a matter of urgency. In haste, Sean replied to this email, placing an order for ‘10,000 mugs at €3 per mug as per previous discussions’. Tina replied by email the next morning confirming the agreement at a total cost of €30,000. Sean, realising his mistake, emailed Tina stating ‘I of course meant 1000 mugs at €3 and 9000 mugs at €2 as per your previous quote’. Tina insists the deal at €30,000 stands. Sean also ordered 500 t-shirts with the image of popular Irish Olympic athlete Barney McScruffles in the front. The t-shorts cost €5000 including a €2000 deposit which Sean paid in advance. However, in July, before the t-shirts were delivered, Barney McScruffles failed a drug test and was banned from competing in the Olympics. Sean wishes to cancel his contract for the t-shirts as he believes nobody will now want to buy them. He believes the contract has been frustrated and wants to get his deposit back. Tina is insisting that Sean accept delivery of the t-shorts and pay the remaining €3000 she says is due. Advise Sean.

questions.description: Lucy is a wedding planner. She charges couples a set fee for an entire wedding package and then enters into sub-contract…
questions.text: Lucy is a wedding planner. She charges couples a set fee for an entire wedding package and then enters into sub-contracts with hairdressers, caterers and other wedding providers. Last month she hired a hairdresser, Claude, for a particularly extravagant wedding for two Irish celebrities. One of the reasons she hired Claude was that he told her that he had 2 years experience working as a hairdresser in a top Dublin hairdressing salon, Classy Cuts. Lucy paid a €500 deposit to Claude and agreed to pay a further €500 on the day of the wedding. However, Lucy has just discovered that although Claude is a qualified hairdresser, in fact he worked in Classy Cuts washing hair and did not actually cut or style any hair when there. She is furious at Claude for misleading her like this. She wishes to cancel her contract with Claude before the day of the wedding and recover her deposit. Lucy also entered into a contract with Finn to cater for a small wedding for a couple, John and Jenny in July. The contract stated that Finn would provide a 5 course sit-down meal for 20 people for about €1500.00. However, on the day of the wedding Finn merely provided a buffet worth approximately €500. John and Jenny are very upset that their wedding meal was ruined and are suing Lucy for damages for breach of contract, including mental distress and inconvenience. Lucy wishes to know whether John and Jenny can sue for these damages. She also wants to know whether she can recover damages against Finn, in particular as she feels she was not provided with €1500 worth of food and that her reputation has been affected by the whole incident. Advise Lucy on her legal position vis a vis (a) Claude (b) Finn and (c) John and Jenny.

questions.description: Tom is a photographer and the owner of Beautiful Stars, a modelling agency based in Cork. Three years ago, he agreed to …
questions.text: Tom is a photographer and the owner of Beautiful Stars, a modelling agency based in Cork. Three years ago, he agreed to act as an agent for Helen, a 15 year old model at the start of her career. The contract stated that Tom was to receive a commission of 25% of all of Helen’s income from any fashion shoots or catwalk shows that she works on for the next 10 years. Helen had a very successful 3 years but when she turned 19 she decided that she was unhappy with the arrangement with Tom. She cancelled their contract and joined another modelling agency that she claimed gave her a better deal. Tom wants to sue Helen for breach of contract but Helen says that she is entitled to cancel her contract because she was only 15 when she entered into it. Tom decides to encourage some of the models to take part in a 2 week catwalk training course in Italy. He sends all of his models an email which states: ‘If you take part in the catwalk training, I will reimburse your travel costs and give you €300 for living costs’. One of the models, Sally, decides to take part in the training and travels to Italy for the course. However, when she claims back her expenses and her €300 on her return, Tom refuses to pay her as he says that his email was not legally binding and she never told him in advance that she was going to take part in the training. Finally, Tom recently hired Molly a local photographer to take professional quality headshots of some of the models in the agency, for an agreed fee of €2000 payable in advance. The agreement was reached over the phone and no written contract was drawn up or signed. However, when the photographs arrive Tom is very unhappy with them as the majority of the pictures are slightly out of focus. When he asks molly for his money back, she refuses to reimburse him. Advise Tom on his legal position vis a vis (a) Helen (b) Sally and (c) Molly.

questions.description: Max runs a successful dog minding business. Since he started the business 5 years ago, he has ordered dog food from the …
questions.text: Max runs a successful dog minding business. Since he started the business 5 years ago, he has ordered dog food from the same dog food distribution company, Gruff Ltd. At the start of every month, Max phones Gruff Ltd and orders 20 bags of luxury dog food for which Max pays for by transferring money online into Gruff Ltd’s bank account. However, recently Max decided to try a different dog food so last month he phoned Gruff Ltd and told them he wouldn’t be ordering from them anymore. Gruff Ltd’s sales representative, Lucille, told  Max that they have an ‘ongoing contractual arrangement’ and that ‘he cannot just cancel the contract without notice’. She says that it is implied from their previous dealings that he should give 3 months notice of any intention to terminate the contract. Max does not believe he had a contract, as he prefers to keep things informal. In addition, in April 2016, Max entered into a 1 year written contract with an internet advertising company, Positive Publicity Ltd (PP Ltd). PP Ltd agreed to design and maintain a new website for Max and to produce a series of videos about his dog minding business which would be on the website and hopefully go ‘viral’ on the internet, increasing publicity for Max. Work on the contract was due to begin in June with the website and first video going online in July. A fee of €6000 was agreed, payable in monthly instalments of €500. However, in May 2016, Melanie, Max’s daughter, finished her university course in website and video production and offered to design and maintain the website and make the video for free. Max contacted PP Ltd and said that he was cancelling the contract. However, PP Ltd refused to accept this cancellation and stated that they would continue with the design work and production of the video and claim the fees owed. Advise Max on his legal position vis a vis (a) Gruff Ltd and (b) PP Ltd.

questions.description: On Monday, Dara phones Sharon, a car dealer, to discuss the possible purchase of her Aston Martin car. Dara promises to …
questions.text: On Monday, Dara phones Sharon, a car dealer, to discuss the possible purchase of her Aston Martin car. Dara promises to pay Sharon €120,000 for the car, payment upon delivery. Sharon is delighted and promises to deliver the car the following Friday. However, the next day, Dara phones Sharon to inform her that his new age spiritual counsellor Mo has advised him that his interest in the car is pathetic and part of a mid-life crisis. As a consequence, Dara tells Sharon that he wishes to revoke his offer. He claims that as the offer was a unilateral one and she has not yet performed ‘her part of the deal’ or provided any consideration, he is entitled to do so. Sharon announces her intention to sue him for breach of contract. Dara, again on Mo’s advice, agrees to transfer ownership of his highly successful PR company to Mo, believing that it will be used to help Mo disseminate his message of self fulfilment through philanthropy to a wider audience. Dara is advised on the transaction by Hugh, a solicitor who is Mo’s neighbour. One week after the transfer is complete, Dara decides that he has been unwise and seeks to avoid the agreement with Mo. Mo is pressing Dara to execute a deed of transfer. Advise Dara.

questions.description: Sinead is a farmer with a large plot of land in Kerry. On 2nd August she entered into a contract with Tom whereby Tom ag…
questions.text:
Sinead is a farmer with a large plot of land in Kerry. On 2nd August she entered into a contract with Tom whereby Tom agreed to cut back all of the hedgerows on Sinead’s land including large sections of uncultivated land for an agreed fee of €1000 to be paid on completion. They did not discuss the dates on which the work was to be carried out and Tom cut back the hedgerows on 15th August. However, when Tom sought payment 
Sinead refused to pay. Sinead claimed that as under the Wildlife Act 1976, as amended, it is an offence to cut hedges between 1st March and 31st August each year, it was illegal for Tom to perform the contract in August and she is under no obligation to pay. In addition, in September Sinead purchased a six month old used tractor for her farm from Tractor Supplies Ltd (TS Ltd) for €15,000. When she purchased the tractor she told Max, TS Ltd’s Senior Sales Representative, that she needed the tractor to access fields up steep hills. Max reassured her that the tractor would ‘be great on rough and high terrain’. No documents were signed during the transaction although Sinead receives a short receipt for payment. However, when Sinead takes the tractor out on the farm it is not suitable for going up to the higher fields as it regularly stalls and breaks down going up steep hills. When she complains to Max he says that ‘all sales are final’ and that she has no rights as she is not a consumer. Advise Sinead. For the purposes of this question, please assume that Sinead is not a consumer.

questions.description: As part of her New Year’s Resolutions, Niamh decides that she is going to get fitter and join her local gym, Complete Fi…
questions.text: As part of her New Year’s Resolutions, Niamh decides that she is going to get fitter and join her local gym, Complete Fitness Ltd. When she goes into the gym to join, she signs a 12 month contract. The contract states that the gyms opening hours are 6am to 10pm every day. This suits Niamh as she likes to go to the gym early before work. The contract also provides that Niamh is to pay a monthly charge of €70. The back of the contract lists around 20 terms and conditions in small print but Niamh does not read any of them. For the first month Niamh attends the gym three mornings a week at 6 am before heading into work. However in February a notice is put up in the gym indicating that the opening hours are to change and that the gym will now only be open from 9am to 6pm. Niamh is furious but when she complains to the manager Tom, he points to term number 8 of the contract which states: ‘The management reserves the right to change the opening hours of the gym without notice and for any reason. No liability shall be incurred for any such change’. Niamh decides to take her business elsewhere and tells Tom that she is no longer going to pay the monthly fee. However, Tom says that she signed up to a 12 month contract and he refers to term 9 of the contract which states: ‘This is a 12 month contract. At the end of the 12 months the customer may terminate the contract on the giving of one month’s notice. Any customer who terminates the contract before 12 months must pay the total amount due for the 12 months immediately’ In addition, one week ago Niamh went online to an Irish website –www.eadai.ie- and ordered some t-shirts and leggings to wear whilst exercising. However, when they arrived in the post two days ago she tried them on and didn’t particularly like them, even though they were as described on the website. Niamh wants to know if she has any legal right to return the clothes. Advise Niamh on her rights vis-à-vis Complete Fitness Ltd and Eadai.

questions.description: Saibh owns a catering business. She decides to expand her business so she can cater for more events and so she order 3 m…
questions.text: Saibh owns a catering business. She decides to expand her business so she can cater for more events and so she order 3 more new ovens from Ross, to be delivered on February 10th. She tells Ross that she needs the new ovens because she plans on expanding her business. Ross doesn’t deliver the ovens until April 20th. Saibh is furious. She has missed out on profits which she believes she would have earned from the expansion of her business and she was forced to cancel a particularly lucrative contract she had entered into to cater for a government St Patricks Day party in March. The whole incident is causing her a lot of stress and inconvenience and her doctor prescribes her tranquilisers to help her to cope. Ross admits that he is in breach of contract but refuses to pay damages to Saibh as he says that in the ‘current economic climate’ it is impossible to predict how successful her business would have been and how much money she lost as a result of the late delivery of the ovens. He also says that she could have hired out ovens from another source between February and April. Advise Saibh.

questions.description: In January 2015, Alice commissioned a painting of her pedigree dog Maggie from Pet Portraits Ltd (PP Ltd) for a fee of €…
questions.text: In January 2015, Alice commissioned a painting of her pedigree dog Maggie from Pet Portraits Ltd (PP Ltd) for a fee of €3000.00. The commissioned painting was to be produced from sketches made during the three one-hour observations of Maggie the dog and Alice was to have full ownership rights in the finished product. Alice also agreed to allow PP Ltd to use (free of charge) a digital copy of the image in its promotional material. After Maggie’s three observation sessions, PP Ltd wrote to all its customers indicating that it was in financial difficulties and would be unable to complete the projects at the fee agreed. Alice contacted PP Ltd and offered an extra €1,000 if the portrait was completed. PP Ltd accepted the promise of additional payment and completed the project. Alice was pleased with the final portrait and paid €4,000 on delivery. However, when Maggie won the Woofts dog show in March 2015, Alice withdrew her permission to use the image. PPP Ltd argued that she was unable to do so as they had relied on her promise in spending €5,000 producing promotional material for their business with Maggie’s image on it, within the terms of the licence. Alice is also demanding the return of the extra €1,000 paid as she claims that she received ‘nothing extra’ in return for this money.

questions.description: Finn books Joes theatre to host a concert for his wine club at a total cost of €4,000. Finn informs Joe of his plans to …
questions.text: Finn books Joes theatre to host a concert for his wine club at a total cost of €4,000. Finn informs Joe of his plans to seat 500 people for the event and that he intends to provide food and wine to the entire audience. Joe accepts a €1,000 deposit and they agree that the remaining €3,000 will be paid on the morning of the concert. Two days before the concert, Finn’s application for a wine licence for the event is turned down by the District Court on the grounds that Finn had not completed all the relevant paper work to accompany the application. Finn tells Joe that their contract is frustrated and that he is treating the contract as at an end. Joe disputes this and argues that the contract is valid but that he is treating Finn’s statement as a repudiatory breach. Joe has discovered that Finn is delighted with the District Courts outcome as he was having difficulties getting wine for the event as his suppliers had declined to supply Finn on credit due to payment arrears. Advise Joe.

questions.description: Talbot Ltd is a small accountancy business in Dublin which has recently expanded and increased its staff. Its managing d…
questions.text: Talbot Ltd is a small accountancy business in Dublin which has recently expanded and increased its staff. Its managing director, Ciara, decides that the Company needs to purchase ten computers for the new employees. She visits Mega Computers Ltd and explains that the computers must be suitable to allow employees to use complex accountancy software programmes. The salesman, Max, tells Ciara that the ‘IRL 2015’ computer will be suitable for complex accountancy software and that it is ‘speedy, reliable and easy to use’. Convinced by this, Ciara immediately orders 10 ‘IRL 2015’ computers. When the computers are delivered, Ciara is given a document headed ‘standard business purchase from’ which she signs and returns to the delivery man. Unfortunately, when the employers at Talbot Ltd begin to sue their computers, it transpires that the new computers are all very slow as the computer processors are all underpowered. As a result, employees cannot efficiently use the accountancy software on them. In addition, 3 out of 10 of the computers have blurry screens and cannot be used at all. Ciara is furious and wishes to return the computers and get her money back. However, when she complains to Max, he points to Mega Computers standard business form which states: ‘All sales are final and all statutory warranties and conditions are excluded. The seller is not liable for any loss caused by any defect in any product whatsoever’. Advise Ciara.

questions.description: Ben is a plumber but he decided to change career and follow his lifelong dream of going to university to study law. To f…
questions.text: Ben is a plumber but he decided to change career and follow his lifelong dream of going to university to study law. To find his studies, he offers to sell his van and its contents to his friend Paula who is planning on setting up her own plumbing business. Paula agrees to buy the van and its contents for €5,000. However, one week after delivering the van to Paula, Ben realises he accidentally left a small box of antique jewellery in the van. This box of jewellery is worth approximately €3,000 he immediately phones Paula to claim it back. However, Paula refuses to hand back the jewellery saying that Ben agreed to sell the ‘van and its contents’ including the antique jewellery. Ben says that Paula must have realised that he made a mistake and did not intend to include the antique box with the van. Two weeks later, Paula’s apprentice Paddy is injured when the van veers off the road and crashes into a lamp post. An accident report confirms that the accident was caused by a serious defect in the vans steering. Advise Ben on his legal situation vis-á-vis Paula and on his possible liability to Paddy under Irish Statute law.

questions.description: On Monday, Paul, a jewellery shop owner places a necklace in his shop window with the following sign: ‘classic 1930’s go…
questions.text: On Monday, Paul, a jewellery shop owner places a necklace in his shop window with the following sign: ‘classic 1930’s gold necklace, €1500 or nearest offer’ On Tuesday, Joan sees the necklace in the front window and goes into the shop saying to Paul ‘I will give you €900 for it’. Paul replies ‘I cannot go that low but I will sell it to you for €1200.00’. Joan is not sure if she is willing to pay this much so she asks Paul if she can have until Saturday to decide. Paul agrees not to sell the necklace to anyone else before Saturday but leaves the necklace and sign in the window. On Wednesday, Joan decides that she is willing to pay €1,200 and phones Paul at his jewellery shop. Since there is no reply, Joan leaves a message on Paul’s answering machine, saying that she will buy the necklace for €1,200. Unfortunately although the machine records the message, Paul’s cleaner accidentally deletes all the messages before Paul hears them. On Friday morning, Joan’s friend Sharon goes into Paul’s jewellery shop and offers Paul €1,400 for the necklace. Delighted by this offer and entirely forgetting about Joan, Paul immediately accepts. However, when Joan and Sharon meet that afternoon, Sharon shows Joan her new necklace. Joan is furious as she believes she has a contract with Paul not to sell to anyone else as well as a fully formed contract of sale for the necklace itself. She immediately goes down to Pauls shop to complain and threatens to sue Paul for breach of contract. Advise Paul.

questions.description: Sam is a keen follower of rugby and a Munster supporter. He is the owner of a rugby ball signed by the Munster team that…
questions.text:
Sam is a keen follower of rugby and a Munster supporter. He is the owner of a rugby ball signed by the Munster team that beat the All-Blacks in 1978. Having recently fallen on hard times, he decides to sell the ball and advertises it in the Rugby Times. Several weeks later, Sam receives a letter containing an offer to buy the ball for €5,000. It is sent be a person claiming to be Nicola Ward, a descendant of one of the signatories of the ball. Nicola requests that the ball be sent before payment so that the signature son it can be verified. Sam verifies with the Irish Rugby Union that the descendant of one of the 1978 team is indeed named Nicola Ward. He sends the ball to the address given in the letter with an invoice addressed to Nicola Ward for €5,000. Several days, later he receives a cheque for 
€5,000 signed by Nicola Ward but the bank refuses to honour the cheque. When he tries to phone Nicola the telephone line has been disconnected and further enquiries reveal no one named Nicola Ward at the address given. The writer of the letter is in fact a person named Naomi who makes the practice of cheating people out of sporting memorabilia and uses several addresses and contacts as a means of carrying out her frauds. A few days later Naomi sells the ball to Conor, a collector of sporting memorabilia for €4,000 paid in cash as requested. Sam has managed to trace the ball to Conor and wishes to recover the ball from him. Advise Sam.

questions.description: Beta Ltd provides computer maintenance and repair services to small businesses in Dublin. In April 2014, Beta Ltd enters…
questions.text: Beta Ltd provides computer maintenance and repair services to small businesses in Dublin. In April 2014, Beta Ltd enters into a contract with Accounts Ltd, a small accountancy firm whereby Beta Ltd agrees to service and repair all the computers used by Accounts Ltd for a period of 2 years from April 2014 for a fixed price per month. Term 6 of the contract states: ‘Term 6: Beta Ltd will service all the computers at the premises of Accounts Ltd every Tuesday from April 2014 to March 2016 and carry out any necessary repairs or maintenance. This obligation will not apply on any public holidays that fall on a Tuesday for the period stated’. From April 2014 to January 2015, Beta Ltd provide this service as agreed and Accounts Ltd is happy with the performance. However in February 2015, Beta Ltd’s computer engineers fail to show up without giving any reason on two consecutive Tuesdays. Two computers break down during this period and it is not possible to use complex accountancy software on them for over a week. Accounts Ltd estimates that it lost approximately €2,000 due to this. In addition, Accounts Ltd has recently learned that another computer maintenance and repair service company, Alpha Ltd, could supply the same service as Beta Ltd but at a lower cost. Accounts Ltd wants to hire Alpha Ltd instead and wants to know if it can rely on the failure to perform in February 2015 to validly terminate its contract with Beta Ltd. Accounts Ltd also wants to claim damages for the losses it suffered in February 2015. Advise Accounts Ltd.

questions.description: Freezer Ltd is a transport company that specialises in the transport of frozen and refrigerated goods across Ireland. Ka…
questions.text:
Freezer Ltd is a transport company that specialises in the transport of frozen and refrigerated goods across Ireland. Kate, the manager of Medical Drugs Galway Ltd (MDG Ltd) regularly hires Freezer Ltd to transport pharmaceutical drugs from MDG Ltd’s warehouse in Galway to hospitals and pharmacies across the country. When she needs a batch of drugs transported, Kate simply phones Ross, the manager of Freezer Ltd and he sends her over a refrigerated lorry. On three or four occasions in the past Kate has emphasised to Ross the importance of keeping the pharmaceutical drugs at a temperature of no higher than 2 degrees Celsius. When the lorries arrive at the warehouse to collect the drugs, Kate fills in a form headed ‘delivery details’ specifying the destination and contents of the delivery and signing it at the bottom. In January 2014, Kate phones Ross to send over a lorry to transport drugs from the warehouse to Cork and signs the ‘delivery details’ form as per usual. However, on this occasion Freezer Ltd’s driver fails to set up the refrigeration system correctly and the drugs are stored at a temperature of 10 degrees Celsius. As a result the drugs are all damaged and have to be thrown away, causing MDG Ltd a loss of €100,000. However, when Kate complains to Ross he points to the ‘delivery details’ form which at the bottom in capital letters says: ‘ALL DELIVERIES ARE SUBJECT TO FREEZER LTD’S TERMS AND CONDITIONS WHICH CAN 
BE FOUND ON OUR WEBSITE’. Ross tells Kate that Term 3 of the terms and conditions of transport to the website states: ‘Freezer Ltd is not responsible in tort or contract for any damage caused to any products transported owing to any technical failure in the refrigeration system whether caused by negligence or otherwise’. Kate wishes to sue Freezer Ltd for damages for the loss suffered and wants to know about the exclusion clause. Advise Kate.
