questions.description: Máire decides to sell her car and buy a new one. She knows her friend Jim is interested in her car, so she emails him, o…
questions.text: Máire decides to sell her car and buy a new one. She knows her friend Jim is interested in her car, so she emails him, offering to sell him her car for €5,000, “the offer to be kept open until noon July 11th”. She says that if Jim wants to buy the car, he should "indicate acceptance via email and pay a deposit of €500 before the deadline". Jim emails Máire to accept her offer on July 8th but does not send on a deposit. On July 9th Máire drops by Jim's house to tell him that she is revoking her offer and the car is no longer available to buy as she has agreed to sell it to her friend Sarah. Jim objects, saying that Máire impliedly promised not to sell the car to anyone else before noon on June 11th, and that he has already accepted her offer and she cannot now revoke it. Jim also tries to give her the €500 deposit in cash but Máire refuses to accept this. Jim is threatening to sue Máire for breach of contract. Máire goes shopping for a new car. She visits her local car dealer, Andy, and sees a car she likes in the forecourt, with a price tag of €8,000. However when she goes into the shop to buy the car, Andy tells her that there was a mistake on the price tag and the car actually costs €10,000. Maeve wants to know if she can insist on Andy selling her the car for €8,000. Advise Máire.

questions.description: Terry is a builder. In May 2014 he builds his neighbour Max a new garden shed as a surprise birthday present. Max is del…
questions.text: Terry is a builder. In May 2014 he builds his neighbour Max a new garden shed as a surprise birthday present. Max is delighted when he sees the completed shed and says “I insist on paying you a grand for this work.” However, he fails to pay Terry any money, and when Terry asks him for the money Max says that he doesn't have a legally binding obligation to pay him the €1,000. In June 2014, Terry completes a building contract for Emma and requests payment of the outstanding balance of €200,000. His contract with Emma states that this sum is due upon completion. By August 2014, Emma still has not paid, claiming she has "cash flow" problems, and Terry is in desperate financial straits. He requests that Emma pay him €150,000 immediately “in full settlement” of their account. Emma agrees to this and immediately pays him this amount, by cheque. The cheque has been cashed but Terry subsequently sues Emma for the outstanding balance plus interest. Emma argues that Terry is estopped from making such a claim but Terry insists that his promise to accept €150,000 in full settlement of the debt is unenforceable. Advise Terry.

questions.description: Felix is a struggling artist who works from his home, a house which he inherited from his grandmother. He is severely de…
questions.text: Felix is a struggling artist who works from his home, a house which he inherited from his grandmother. He is severely depressed and on strong anti-depressant medication which leaves him drowsy. Felix’s boyfriend Sam runs a music shop in town, but Felix does not know that the shop is rapidly losing money. Sam wants to take out a business loan of €100,000 to cover his losses, but needs Felix to act as guarantor and to charge his interest in the home as security for the loan. Sam tells Felix that the loan is simply to cover the cost of an extension which he is building on the premises, and that it is a loan of €10,000. Felix is very insecure about their relationship, and is worried that Sam is about to leave him. He agrees to act as guarantor, and charges his interest in the home to the bank as security. In the bank he signs a form which states that he has received independent advice from a solicitor that the loan is for €100,000, but Felix is flustered and does not read over the details of the loan. A year later Sam’s business fails completely and he disappears. The bank is attempting to take possession of Felix’s house. Advise Felix.

questions.description: Natalia runs a small business in which she takes groups of tourists out onto a local lake for short cruises.  She buys a…
questions.text: Natalia runs a small business in which she takes groups of tourists out onto a local lake for short cruises.  She buys a second hand boat from Andy, and during the course of negotiations she mentions that she will be using the boat for this purpose. However, one day, a month after buying the boat, the engine appears to malfunction - it becomes very loud and starts to emit fumes and black smoke. Her clients begin to complain and some start to look for their money back after the trip. The fumes are so bad that she is forced to stop using the boat and, as she cannot get a replacement, she has to cancel several lake cruises which had been booked. When she asks Andy to repair the boat, he says that the boat was old and that he never warranted that the boat would be of satisfactory quality. Natalia wants Andy to repair the boat, or failing that to give her a refund. She also wants damages to compensate for the tours which were cancelled and those where she had to provide a refund. Advise Natalia.

questions.description: Aoife is an internationally recognised collector of Waterford crystal. She reads in the magazine "Crystal" of an auction…
questions.text: Aoife is an internationally recognised collector of Waterford crystal. She reads in the magazine "Crystal" of an auction of "fine crystal" at Kelly's Auctions in Dublin. She attends the viewing the day before the auction. She meets Seán, a recently retired glass-blower in the Waterford crystal factory. Seán explains his background and tells her that in order to bolster his pension he has decided to sell his entire personal collection of glasses in one lot, Lot 17. Aoife inspects Lot 17 and is delighted with the quality of the collection. She is unaware that at least half of the glasses are not Waterford crystal but are imitations mass produced in China. Seán's son bought them for Seán as a joke during one of his trips to the East. Seán is aware of Aoife's mistake but ensures that he does not offer more than "fine crystal" in general terms. When Aoife tells him sympathetically that it must be hard to part with "his life's work", Seán responds merely by saying ''No, it is not". Aoife buys Lot 17 which is advertised merely as "Assorted Glassware". Six months later Aoife discovers the true origins of the Chinese glasses and claims that either the contract with Seán is invalid as a result of this mistake and that she is entitled to her money back, or that Seán is liable for failing to disclose the fact that the glasses were not all Waterford crystal. Aoife also bids €200 for a Waterford crystal chandelier owned by Tom. Although the chandelier is advertised as "without reserve", it is withdrawn from the auction when hers is the only bid. Kelly, the auctioneer, is acting on Tom's instructions. Two years later, Aoife reads that the chandelier has been sold for €40,000. She wishes to sue Torn for breach of contract. Advise Aoife.

questions.description: Ethan owns a business which produces organic jams and soups. He enters into a contract with Catherine, a local farmer, f…
questions.text: Ethan owns a business which produces organic jams and soups. He enters into a contract with Catherine, a local farmer, for the purchase of 100 crates of organic fruit at a cost of €10,000. Clause 8 of the signed written contract states: "The buyer herby waives any implied statutory warranty of merchantability or of fitness for a particular purpose. In the event that any fruit sold is not of merchantable quality or fit for a particular purpose, we will refund all payments made to us by the buyer and this shall be the limit of our obligation. We hereby exclude all liability for any loss or damage arising from the use of any fruit supplied by us and for any consequential loss or damage arising out of such use." Catherine inadvertently sprays her fruit crop with chemical pesticides. When Ethan uses the fruit his jam is contaminated and he suffers a loss of €25,000 as a result. When Ethan sues Catherine for breach of contract, Catherine seeks to rely on Clause 8 to limit the damages payable to the €10,000 paid for the fruit. Each month for the last 5 years, Ethan has placed an order for organic vegetables with another local farmer, Paul. There is no express written contract between them but Ethan has told Paul on several occasions that he is "delighted with the arrangement" and that he "looks forward to developing the relationship in future years". Ethan has recently discovered that he can obtain organic vegetables at a lower price from another farmer and wishes to know if he can terminate the arrangement with Paul. Advise Ethan.

questions.description: Transport Shipping Ltd hired a ship to Grains Ltd in June 2013 for a hire fee of €2,500 per day. The ship was due to be …
questions.text: Transport Shipping Ltd hired a ship to Grains Ltd in June 2013 for a hire fee of €2,500 per day. The ship was due to be returned on 31st August 2013 and so Transport Shipping Ltd arranged to hire the same ship out to a third party, McKenna Ltd, for 90 days from 1st September 2013. Transport Shipping Ltd was able to negotiate an unusually high hire fee of €5,000 per day for this second hire period. Grains Ltd failed to return the ship until 10th September 2013. Grains Ltd admits it is in breach of contract and is willing to pay damages of €27,000 to cover an extra 10 days' hire at the current market rate of €2,700 per day. However as a result of the late delivery of the ship, Transport Shipping Ltd was forced to renegotiate its contract with McKenna Ltd to allow for a delayed start to the hire period and a reduced rate of hire of €2,000 per day. It thus claims that it has lost profits of €3,000 per day for the duration of the contract with McKenna Ltd, and wants damages of €270,000 to compensate for this loss. Grains Ltd argues that there is a "general understanding" in the shipping  industry that damages should be limited to the market rate of hire until the ship is returned, and should not include any liability for any loss of future profits. Grains Ltd also argues that they were unaware and could not reasonably have been aware that Transport Shipping Ltd had entered into such a lucrative contract with McKenna Ltd. To avoid similar situations in the future, Transport Shipping Ltd wants to insert a clause in its contract providing for a set amount of compensation to be paid when there is a delay in returning a ship on time. It seeks your advice on whether and under what circumstances such a clause would be enforced by the courts. Advise Transport Shipping Ltd on its position regarding Grains Ltd and on the possibility of including such a clause in future.

questions.description: Dervla is a graphic designer who is concerned about health issues, in particular the dangers of young people smoking. On…
questions.text: Dervla is a graphic designer who is concerned about health issues, in particular the dangers of young people smoking. On her own initiative she designs a poster for "Tobac'', a charity that works to educate young people about the dangers of smoking. Edward, the public relations officer for Tobac, is delighted with the poster, and in an email to Dervla he writes: "We love your poster! As a token of our gratitude for your hard work, we would like to give you €1,000." Dervla replies "That's great, thank you!" and on the basis of this statement she buys herself a new state of the art laptop computer costing €950. However the next week Edward emails Dervla to say that owing to cutbacks they are no longer able to pay her any money. Dervla wants to know if there is any way she can claim the €1,000 promised. Dervia discovers that her 15 year old niece Anna has started smoking. Dervla tells Anna that if she quits smoking and does not smoke for six months that she will pay her €500. Six months later Anna contacts Dervla to let her know that she has successfully abstained from smoking for six months and that she wants her money. Dervla argues that she wasn't serious when she offered Anna the money; that she has not benefited from Anna's actions; and that it was only a promise to a young family member and not legally binding. She wants to know if she has to pay Anna the €500. Advise Dervla.

questions.text: Answer (a) or (b) Discuss the main changes introduced by the implementation of the Consumer Rights Directive (Directive 2011/83/EU of the European Parliament and of the Council on Consumer Rights) in Ireland. Or Discuss, with reference to case law, the circumstances in which an order of specific performance is an appropriate remedy for breach of contract.

questions.description: Answer (a) and (b) Máire is a slightly overweight woman who sees the following advertisement in the newspaper: "The Shri…
questions.text: Answer (a) and (b) Máire is a slightly overweight woman who sees the following advertisement in the newspaper: "The Shrink Delight Diet - Weight Loss While You Wait! Drink a Shrink Delight Milkshake for Breakfast, a Shrink Delight Soup for Lunch and have a sensible dinner and watch your weight drop! Shrink Delight Ltd is so confident in this product that if you follow the Shrink Delight Diet and don't lose 10 kilograms within 6 weeks, we will give you €2,000!" Máire buys a 6 week supply of Shrink Delight Milkshakes and Shrink Delight Soups. She drinks the milkshake for breakfast and the soup for lunch every day, and has what she regards as a sensible dinner every evening. However, after 6 weeks she has not lost any weight. When she contacts Shrink Delight Ltd, they refuse to give her the €2,000, saying that Máire should not have taken the advertisement seriously. Advise Máire on whether she has a claim in contract against Shrink Delight Ltd for the €2,000. AND James is the manager of Machine Ltd, which is involved in the business of buying and selling large machinery for use in the construction industry. On January 15th, James received a phone call from Todd, the manager of a large construction company, Construction Ltd. Todd asked James to post him a quotation for a XL Super Crane. James posted a letter to Todd, offering to sell the crane for €50,000. The letter stated that the offer was subject to Machine Ltd's terms and conditions, which were included with the letter. Term 6 was a price variation clause which provided that the cost of the machine could be increased by up to 10% in the event of changes in market conditions. Todd replied to this offer by sending James an order form by post, using Construction Ltd's own standard order form. The price of the crane was stated to be €50,000, but the order form contained its own set of terms and conditions, which did not contain a price variation clause. James signed and returned by post a portion of Construction Ltd's order form which acknowledged that the contract took place on the purchaser's terms. However, he included a covering letter which stated that the sale was to take place on Machine Ltd's terms and conditions. The XL Crane was delivered on March 15th and James sent an invoice for €55,000, referring to a change in market conditions and Term 6 of the contract as an explanation for the increase in price.  Construction Ltd is refusing to pay the extra €5,000. Advise James.

questions.description: John goes shopping in Dublin city centre every Saturday and regularly parks his car in the O'Connell Street car park in …
questions.text: John goes shopping in Dublin city centre every Saturday and regularly parks his car in the O'Connell Street car park in the centre of Dublin. This is a "Pay and Display'' car park, meaning that each week John first parks his car, then pays for his parking at a machine and receives a parking ticket, which he leaves visible in the window of his car before going shopping. There is a sign above the pay machine headed "Terms and Conditions of Parking". The sign contains 20 clauses in relatively small print. The 12th term excludes liability "in contract or tort or any other action" for "any damage to property or persons howsoever caused" while cars are left in the car park. One Saturday John parks his car in the O'Connell Street car park and pays for his ticket as normal, but when he returns to his car a large light falls from the ceiling of the car park onto his car. John sustains cuts and bruises, and the windscreen of his car is smashed. It transpires that the light fell as a result of the negligence of the car park staff. However, Carla, the Manager of the car park, claims that John cannot bring an action against the owners of the car park in either contract or tort for his injuries or the damage to his car because of clause 12 of the "Terms and Conditions of Parking". Advise John on whether clause 12 has been incorporated into his contract, and, assuming it is incorporated, whether it can prevent him from bringing an action against the owners of the car park.

questions.description: Jane runs a catering business from her home, and Christmas is one of her busiest times of year as she caters for a lot o…
questions.text: Jane runs a catering business from her home, and Christmas is one of her busiest times of year as she caters for a lot of office Christmas parties.   In early October she orders 50 turkeys from Pat, a local turkey farmer.  The turkeys are to be delivered on 1st December.  Jane explains to Pat that she always serves turkey as the main course at Christmas parties.  However, on 1st December Pat phones to say that he won't be able to deliver the turkeys.  Jane is distraught.  She cannot get turkeys from any other source, because it is too late in the season.  She has to cancel 10 parties. She would have made a final profit of €3,000 had she not had to cancel these parties. As a result of cancelling these parties she also cannot make use of other ingredients she has purchased and she has wasted advertising costs.  In total she has a wasted expenditure of €2,000.  The whole incident is causing her a lot of stress and inconvenience, and early in the New Year her doctor prescribes her tranquilisers to help her cope.  Jane wants damages to cover her lost profits and her wasted expenditure, as well as damages to cover the stress and inconvenience.  Pat admits that he is in breach of contract, but refuses to pay damages to Jane. To help her relax after this ordeal, Jane decides to have a Jacuzzi installed in her home.  She enters into a contract with Tony, whereby Tony agrees to supply and install a Jacuzzi three metres long and one metre deep in Jane's bathroom, at a cost of €1,000. However, when the Jacuzzi is installed, Jane is annoyed because it is only two and a half metres long and ninety centimetres deep. Other than the difference in size, the Jacuzzi is perfectly useable and has all of the features Jane requires. Jane wants damages of €3,000 to cover the cost of tearing out the Jacuzzi that has been installed and replacing it with a properly installed, bigger Jacuzzi. She wants further damages for the stress and inconvenience of getting the Jacuzzi replaced. Tony admits that he is in breach of contract, but he is refusing to pay damages. He says that the difference in the size of the Jacuzzi is only minor, and that the Jacuzzi installed is still worth at least €900. He suspects that Jane has no intention of actually replacing the installed Jacuzzi but instead wants to use the money to go on holidays. Advise Jane.

questions.description: In January 2008, Mel rents an office from Well Ltd agreeing to pay an annual rent of €55,000 subject to a rent review on…
questions.text: In January 2008, Mel rents an office from Well Ltd agreeing to pay an annual rent of €55,000 subject to a rent review on 30th December 2013.  The rent review clause provides that on that date the rent should reflect the current market rental value as agreed between parties. In December 2013 the market rental value of the office is €80,000. Finn, a senior manager in Well Ltd, writes to Mel suggesting a figure of €52,000 for the new rent. Mel writes back readily agreeing to renew upon the terms proposed. Finn then realises his mistake and writes to Mel explaining that he had intended to propose a rent of €85,000 and asking her to treat his letter as if it had suggested a review at that figure. Mel informs Finn that she will not agree to the correction proposed and that she considers that a binding agreement exists. While accepting that Finn has authority to act on its behalf, Well Ltd argues that Finn's mistake invalidates the agreement. Well Ltd is unhappy with Finn's performance and it decides his annual salary of €50,000 could be put to better use. In January 2013, Finn is dismissed and Well Ltd pays him compensation of €150,000. In February 2013, it is discovered that Finn has engaged in insider dealing in breach of his service contract and in breach of company law. It becomes clear that at the time it terminated his contract, Well Ltd could have dismissed Finn for breach without having to pay compensation. Well Ltd argues that the termination contract is void and the compensation repayable. Advise Well Ltd. Please note that your answer should be limited to the contractual issues which arise in this question.

questions.text: Answer (a) or (b) Discuss the effect of frustration of contract. In your answer discuss (i) whether any money paid in advance of the frustrating event must be returned and (ii) whether any work done before the frustrating event must be paid for. OR Discuss the circumstances in which a contract will be declared invalid on the grounds of economic duress.

questions.description: Harry owns an antique watch, a set of antique dishes and some antique coins that he decides to sell. On Monday Harry con…
questions.text: Harry owns an antique watch, a set of antique dishes and some antique coins that he decides to sell. On Monday Harry contacts a local jeweller, Brenda, to offer sell to her the antique watch for €150. Although Brenda is not overly interested in the watch, Harry tells her that he won't sell it to anyone else before Thursday to give Brenda time to think about it. However, on Tuesday Harry meets his friend Chloe, who says that she will pay Harry €150 for the watch. Harry is delighted and immediately accepts. That evening Harry emails Brenda via her website to tell her that he has sold the watch to somebody else. Brenda does not check her emails outside working hours and does not see the email. On Wednesday morning on the way to work Brenda decides that she wants to buy the watch and immediately phones Harry to tell him this. When Harry responds that he has already sold the watch, Brenda is furious and says that ''they had a deal" that he would keep his offer open until Thursday. Brenda is threatening to take legal action against Harry. On Tuesday Harry hears that Marie, a friend of one of his work colleagues, is a collector of antique dishes and plates, and that she might be interested in buying his plates. In a brief set of emails Marie promises to pay Harry €70 for the dishes, payment upon delivery, and Harry promises to deliver the dishes the following Saturday. However the next day Harry phones Marie to tell her he has changed his mind and no longer wants to sell the dishes. He claims that as Marie has not yet performed ''her part of the deal" or provided any consideration, he is entitled to do so. Marie wants to sue Harry for breach of contract. The next weekend Harry puts the antique coins up for auction without any reserve price. At the beginning of the auction the auctioneer states that all sales are ''without reserve unless otherwise stated". Deirdre bids €50 for the coins, and seems to be the only bidder interested in them. Harry isn't happy with this as he thinks the coins are worth at least €200. He instructs the auctioneer to withdraw the coins from the auction, and the auctioneer does this. Deirdre is threatening to sue both Harry and the auctioneer. Advise Harry.

questions.description: Sarah is a vet and owns her own veterinary surgery. Last March she decided to expand her business and she hired Jeff to …
questions.text: Sarah is a vet and owns her own veterinary surgery. Last March she decided to expand her business and she hired Jeff to build a fully completed extension on to the surgery. The contract between Sarah and Jeff clearly stated that the work was to be finished by June 30th, and a penalty clause stated if the work was not completed by this date that Jeff was liable to pay €500 per day until the extension was fully complete. In April, Jeff entered into a sub­contract with Mark, a local plumber who agreed to carry out all the plumbing work on the extension by June 15th, at a fixed price of €5,000. At the time of contracting, Jeff emphasised that it was essential that this work be done on time, saying that it was going to cost him "a fortune" if he was late in completing the work for Sarah. At the start of June Mark contacted Jeff to say that the work was taking longer than he had anticipated. He said that he was going to need an extra payment of €2,000 to enable him to hire extra workers so he could finish the job on time. Jeff reluctantly paid the money and Mark managed to finish on time.  However, once the work was done Jeff wrote Mark a letter demanding the return of the additional €2,000 paid, saying that it was not part of the original agreed price and that Mark had "done nothing extra for the cash". In addition, one of Sarah's clients, Janice, a local farmer, owes Sarah €2,000 in veterinary fees. In May Janice approached Sarah and said that she couldn't afford to pay her the full amount due, but that she'd pay her €800. Janice said Sarah could accept that "or get nothing'', so Sarah reluctantly accepted the lesser amount Sarah now regrets this decision, and wants to know if she can sue Janice for the difference of €1,200. Advise Jeff and Sarah vis-à-vis their respective rights against Mark and Janice.

questions.description: In April Ted sells his ice cream van, complete with ice cream making equipment, to Carla. During negotiations Ted says t…
questions.text: In April Ted sells his ice cream van, complete with ice cream making equipment, to Carla. During negotiations Ted says that last summer he made €7,000 selling ice cream and that next summer is due to be hot so Carla should make a profit of €9,000. He offers to show Carla the accounts but Carla says she's "not great with numbers" and that she will ''take his word for it". However, a month after purchasing the van Carla discovers from a friend in the ice cream trade that Ted hadn't used his ice cream van at all the previous summer, so had made no profit at all. Furthermore her friend tells her that it's very unlikely that she will make a profit of €9,000 over the summer, as the average summer profit for an ice cream van is €5,000. Carla is furious and regrets buying the ice cream van. Late last May Carla asked you to advise her if she can return the ice cream van to Ted and get her money back. After her experience with the ice cream van, Carla decides that the best way to make money over the summer is to set up a surfing school in Cork. She is a surfing expert and has designed a new type of surf board especially for beginners. Leonard is shown the design and in an email is asked by Carla to manufacture 20 copies of the board for €2,000. Leonard replies by email, stating that the surfboards will be "slightly smaller than discussed" and is he "happy to do the job for €200". Carla immediately phones Leonard and asks him to confirm the details in the email. Leonard is on his way to a meeting and replies ''I'm sure the details are correct'' without checking. Carla immediately emails an acceptance agreeing to purchase the surf boards at a total price of €200. Leonard is now refusing to manufacture the surf boards for €200. Carla wants to know if she has a binding contract with Leonard. Advise Carla.

questions.description: Aoife is the manager of a construction site. She needs a large crane and a digger for the site. In January she enters in…
questions.text: Aoife is the manager of a construction site. She needs a large crane and a digger for the site. In January she enters into a contract to hire an ''XL Super Crane" from Tom for one year. Clause 8 of this contract states: ''It is a term of this contract that the XL Super Crane will be of satisfactory quality for the duration of the hire period." One morning in March, the XL Super Crane suddenly stops working. When Aoife phones Tom to complain, he says that it is a minor problem with the engine and that he will need to take the crane away for one week to repair it. He says that he will waive the hire fee for that week. The crane is repaired, but when Tom returns it to the site one week later Aoife refuses to accept it. She says that Tom is in breach of Clause 8 of the contract and that she is entitled to terminate the hire contract. However, Tom suspects that Aoife simply wants out of the contract as the market for plant hire has fallen and she may have found a cheaper crane elsewhere. Aoife purchases a digger for the construction site from Sam. The written contract of sale is brief and makes no mention of the quality of the digger. However, after one day the digger breaks down and won't work. Aoife wants to return the digger to Sam and get her money back. However, Sam says that he gave no warranty or guarantee about the quality of the digger and that she is "stuck with it now''. Advise Aoife on her legal position in relation to both Tom and Sam in relation to the defective equipment.

questions.description: Answer (a) or (b) "An agent has the power to bind their principal where they have "authority'' to act on the principal's…
questions.text: Answer (a) or (b) "An agent has the power to bind their principal where they have "authority'' to act on the principal's behalf and a distinction is drawn between the various types of authority." Outline how agency operates as an exception to the privity rule and the different types of authority which an agent may have to act on the principal's behalf. Or Provide a critical assessment of the Law Reform Commission's Report on Privity of Contract (2008).

questions.description: Claire regularly drinks "low fat soy cappuccinos" at Donal's Place, her local coffee shop. On 29th July when she goes in…
questions.text: Claire regularly drinks "low fat soy cappuccinos" at Donal's Place, her local coffee shop. On 29th July when she goes in and orders her drink, she is told by Donal that this is the millionth cup of coffee sold there. Donal asks whether Claire, as their "millionth customer", is willing to do some publicity work for the shop, in return for a "lifelong supply of free coffee" in the shop.   Claire agrees, and poses for lots of photographs, generating lots of publicity for the coffee shop.  She continues to receive free "low fat soy cappuccinos" every day for the next few months. However, on 2nd January when she goes in and orders her usual drink, she is told that from now on she will only be allowed regular coffees, and that she will be limited to one coffee a week.  Claire believes this is a breach of contract, but Donal argues that that the reference to a lifelong supply of coffee was a mere "advertising puff' which Claire should not have believed. He also says that even if it was an offer, it was simply a gratuitous gift and not part of any binding contract. Claire is so annoyed with the entire situation that she decides not to drink coffee in Donal's Place any more, but she believes she had a contract with Donal and wants to bring an action against Donal for breach of contract. On hearing Claire's story, her friend Ben phones her to ask whether she would be interested in acquiring his new state of the art coffee machine. He explains that he received the coffee machine as a gift at Christmas but does not drink coffee, and if she doesn't take it he ''would just give it away to charity". Claire is delighted and immediately agrees to take the coffee machine. When she collects the coffee machine the next day she is so happy with it that she promises to give Ben €200 for it when she gets her wages on Wednesday. Ben 'is delighted by this and in the knowledge that he will get €200 on Wednesday he goes out for an expensive dinner that night. 'However, when Claire is paid her wages on Wednesday she does not give Ben any money; 'Ben is now threatening to sue Claire for the €200. Advise Claire on her legal position vis à vis (a) Donal and (b) Ben.

questions.description: The Luxury Ireland Hotel is a premier hotel in Dublin. The hotel has a leisure centre with a swimming pool, and has vari…
questions.text: The Luxury Ireland Hotel is a premier hotel in Dublin. The hotel has a leisure centre with a swimming pool, and has various services for its clients, including the provision of dry cleaning as part of the cost of the room. Tom stays in the hotel in January 2013.  When he arrives at reception he completes and signs, but does not read, a registration form. The form includes the following clause: "6. All members and guests use the leisure facilities and swimming pool at their own risk and the club does not accept responsibility for any harm or injury to a guest howsoever caused." Tom uses the swimming pool that evening. However, due to the negligence of the hotel staff the water has been treated with too high a level of chlorine. This causes Tom's eyes to sting and he develops a painful skin rash. He has to visit his doctor and take several days off work. Tom demands compensation for his injuries, the manager of the hotel claims that clause 6 means that the hotel is not liable for Tom's injuries. Niamh is also staying in the Luxury Ireland Hotel in January 2013. She decides to make use of the hotel's dry cleaning service to get her suit cleaned, as the service is included in the cost of the room. She leaves her suit in a plastic bag labelled "Dry Cleaning" to be taken to be cleaned. In fact, the hotel has sub-contracted with a third party, Clean Clothes Ltd., to do all its dry cleaning; and all the clothes from the hotel are taken there on a daily basis. When Niamh's suit is returned there is a large red stain on the back of the jacket and the trousers are ripped. Niamh is furious, but when she threatens to bring an action against the Luxury Ireland Hotel and Clean Clothes Ltd, the manager of the hotel points to an exclusion clause printed on the "Dry Cleaning" plastic bag. It states: "Luxury Ireland Hotel and its employees and agents are not liable in tort or contract for any damage caused to clothes during the dry cleaning process, howsoever caused." Advise Tom on whether clause 6 will prevent him from bringing an action against Luxury Ireland Hotel. Advise Niamh on whether the exclusion clause on the dry cleaning bag will prevent her from bringing an action against Luxury Ireland Hotel. Advise Niamh on whether the exclusion clause on the dry cleaning bag will prevent her from bringing an action against Clean Clothes Ltd.

questions.description: Louise runs a transport company, with a fleet of 150 lorries. ID September 2012 Louise enters into a contract with Alan …
questions.text: Louise runs a transport company, with a fleet of 150 lorries. ID September 2012 Louise enters into a contract with Alan to transport toys from a warehouse in Cork to toy shops across Ireland over the Christmas period, 15th November to 24th December. The total contract price is €20,000, with €5,000 to be paid in advance on 10th November and the rest due on 5th January.  Alan pays €5,000 on 1Oth November. However on 12th November there is an accidental fire in the toy warehouse and the warehouse and all its contents are destroyed. Alan claims that the contract with Louise is frustrated, and that he is entitled to the return of the €5,000 paid. Louise claims that the lorries are still available to Alan for the entire Christmas period and that she should be able to keep the €5,000 paid and claim the remaining €15,000 due. In 2007, Louise enters into a ten year transport contract with Supermarket Ltd, a major Irish supermarket chain. Louise agrees to transport and deliver goods from Supermarket Ltd.'s warehouses to its supermarkets for a period of ten years, at a fixed price of €2 million per year. However, subsequent to this agreement, political crises in the Middle East and unexpected changes in market conditions result in an increase in the cost of oil and petrol. By 2013 petrol prices in Ireland have increased to such an extent that Louise is performing the contract with Supermarket Ltd at a loss. Louise wants to know if she has any grounds on which she can terminate the transport contract with Supermarket Ltd. In addition, for the last five years Louise has regularly transported furniture around the country for Sean, a furniture manufacturer. They have no written contract. Each time Sean needs furniture delivered he simply contacts Louise and she invariably agrees to transport it for him. This occurs approximately once a month. However, in February 2013 Louise decides that she wants to focus on other aspects of the transport business and that she no longer wants to deliver Sean's furniture. Sean however claims that she cannot simply terminate their "arrangement" without notice. He claims that they have a contract and that there is an implied term that Louise should give him at least six months' notice of her intention to terminate the contract. Louise claims there is no such contract or implied term. Advise Louise on her legal position vis à vis (a) Alan and (b) Supermarket Ltd and (c) Sean.

questions.description: Ann is a world famous Irish actress. In October 2012 she agrees with the film company Irish Movies Ltd to play the lead …
questions.text: Ann is a world famous Irish actress. In October 2012 she agrees with the film company Irish Movies Ltd to play the lead role in a science fiction film called "Alien Attack".  She is to receive a payment of €1 million for making the film. Even before Ann signs the contract Irish Movies Ltd spends €500,000 on producing the movie set. Following on from Ann's signature of the contract the company spends €200,000 on special effects in which Ann's image is manipulated by digital technology. However, shortly after signing the contract, Ann informs Irish Movies Ltd that she will no longer be appearing in the film "Alien Attack". She will instead by starring in a rival film “Vampire Attack''. It is widely reported that her reason for doing so was the prospect of greater future earnings from the Vampire Attack franchise, as she was offered €2 million and 1% of box office takings to appear in that film. Irish Movies Ltd decides that without Ann there is no point going ahead with the film, and therefore cancels production. They predict that if the film had gone ahead with Ann as planned "that they would have made profits of€5 million. They now wish to sue Ann for their lost profits and for their wasted expenditure. They also wish to recover from Ann the additional fee that she received for appearing in "Vampire Attack", as they claim she only obtained this extra profit as a result of a "deliberate and cynical breach" of her original contract. However, Ann says that Irish Movies Ltd could easily have hired another actress to replace her, and that the real reason they cancelled production was that the film script for "Alien Attack" was so terrible that they would not have made any profit from the film had it gone ahead. Advise Irish Movies Ltd.

questions.description: Inspired by the Olympics, Ross decides to exercise more and to become stronger and fitter. On Monday he sees the followi…
questions.text: Inspired by the Olympics, Ross decides to exercise more and to become stronger and fitter. On Monday he sees the following advertisement on a website: “Second-hand treadmill for sale. All offers over €300 considered. If interested please email LauraK@email.ie”.  Ross emails Laura to tell her he is very interested in buying the treadmill, but is only willing to pay €250 for it.  Laura immediately replies, saying that she will not sell the treadmill for less than €300, but that she will not sell it to anyone else before Saturday to give Ross time to think about it.  On Friday afternoon Ross emails Laura to say he will pay €300 for the treadmill “as agreed”. However Laura informs Ross that she already sold the treadmill to another individual, Ben, that morning and so cannot sell it to Ross. Ross wants to know if he has entered into a binding contract for the sale of the treadmill and whether he has a cause of action against Laura for selling the treadmill to Ben before Saturday. On Tuesday Ross hears that Sam, a friend of one of his work colleagues, is selling his mountain bike. After a brief discussion Ross promises to pay Sam €200 for the bike, payment upon delivery, and Sam promises to deliver the bike the following Saturday.  However on Wednesday Sam phones Ross to tell him he has changed his mind and no longer wants to sell his bike. He claims that as the offer was a unilateral one and Ross has not yet performed “his part of the deal” or provided any consideration, he is entitled to do so. Ross wants to sue Sam for breach of contract. Finally, on Wednesday Ross sees the following advertisement in a sports magazine: “Mighty Muscle Protein Drink: Get Strong as You Train! Drink Mighty Muscle twice a day and feel the difference! Guaranteed results after two weeks or we’ll pay you €500!” Excited by the advertisement, Ross buys a month’s supply of Might Muscle and takes it as directed. However, after two weeks it has had no effect. When he contacts Mighty Muscle Ltd they refuse to pay him €500, saying that the advertisement was not a serious offer, and even if there was an offer, Ross did not accept it. Ross wants to know if he can claim his €500. Advise Ross on his legal position vis à vis (a) Laura and (b) Sam and (c) Mighty Muscle Ltd.

questions.description: Caoimhe owns a hairdressing salon which was located in the centre of a midlands town, next door to a car park. She adver…
questions.text: Caoimhe owns a hairdressing salon which was located in the centre of a midlands town, next door to a car park. She advertises the sale of the business and premises in the local newspaper. Alan is looking to open up his own hairdressing salon, and seeing the advertisement, he visits the premises. Caoimhe tells him that the profits of the business are usually about €30,000 per year and that she anticipates that profits will increase by at least 15% in the next year. She offers Alan the opportunity to look at the business accounts, but Alan says he will take her word for it because he is not very good at maths. The day after Alan’s visit, the local authority notifies Caoimhe that in three months’ time the High Street will become a traffic free zone and the car park next to the salon will be closed. Caoimhe decides not to pass this information to Alan because she knows that her customers rely on being able to park close to the salon. The following week Alan enters into a written contract to purchase the premises for €200,000. The contract makes no mention of the profit figures or the proposed closing of the car park. After taking over the premises, Alan spends €5,000 on new internal fittings for the salon. A month later, Alan asks his accountant to read Caoimhe’s accounts and discovers that they show that profits had not exceeded €20,000 in any of the last three years and were on a downward trend. Two months later the High Street becomes a traffic free zone, causing a drastic reduction in trade as clients of the salon can no longer park nearby. As a result Alan wishes he had never bought the salon. Advise Alan on whether he has any cause of action against Caoimhe and on the remedies that may be available to him.

questions.description: Steve is a supplier of oats and grains to the owners of the horses and other livestock. Steve sells 520 sacks of oats to…
questions.text: Steve is a supplier of oats and grains to the owners of the horses and other livestock. Steve sells 520 sacks of oats to Rory, a racehorse trainer. Rory tells Steve that the oats are for his racehorses. They agree in a hastily written contract that the oats are to be delivered in 52 weekly instalments of 10 sacks each. Rory agrees to pay for each instalment of oats as they are delivered. The written agreement makes no mention of the quality of oats. The first nine instalments of oats are delivered without any problems arising.  However after eating the tenth instalments of oats Rory’s horses become ill and require veterinary treatment.  As a result of the illness the racehorse cannot be entered into races for six months. The vet confirms that oats delivered had been contaminated while in storage at Steve’s warehouse. Rory is now suing Steve for breach of contract, arguing that the oats were of poor quality and unsuitable for his racehorses.  Rory seeks damages, including the veterinary fees and the loss of profits during the time the racehorses could not be entered into races. Rory claims that his horses were in prime condition and would have won all their races. In addition, Rory has refused to accept delivery of any further instalments of oats from Steve since his racehorses became ill.  He says that as a result of what occurred he considers the contract at an end and will not take delivery of any further instalments of oats from Steve.  The agreed price under the contract was €10 a sack.  Rory is now paying €20 per sack from an alternative supplier. Advise Steve on (a) whether he is in breach of contract and (b) the extent to which Rory will be successful in claiming damages and (c) whether Rory is entitled to consider the contract at an end.

questions.description: Alison is a self-employed archaeologist. In January she sees a call for tenders for archaeological services in a nationa…
questions.text: Alison is a self-employed archaeologist. In January she sees a call for tenders for archaeological services in a national newspaper. The advertisement states that a private company, Modern Developments Ltd (MD Ltd), is looking for an archaeological survey of a piece of land over a two month period, and welcomes bids from all interested archaeologists by 30th January. The advertisement also states that “the lowest compliant bid will be accepted”. Alison really wants the work, so she sends in her tender on time. Her tender states that she can provide the services for €20,000 “or €1,000 less than any lower bid”. She is subsequently dismayed to learn that her tender was rejected.  She later hears that the successful bidder offered to do the work for €19,000. She believes her bid was the lowest and wants to know if she has a cause of action against MD Ltd. In April she carries out an archaeological survey for Peter, a local businessman, for an agreed price of €10,000.  Peter is to pay Alison by June 15th. However, by the end of June Peter has still not paid Alison for her services. Alison presses for payment, to no avail.  Eventually in August Peter tells Alison that in the “current economic climate” he is not sure he will be able to pay her in full, but he offers her €6,000 in settlement of the debt. He tells her that if this offer is not accepted then nothing will be paid.  Alison is worried about her cash flow, and reluctantly accepts Peter’s offer.  Peter pays Alison €6,000. A month later Alison changes her mind and looks for the balance, €4,000, from Peter.  However, Peter says they had a binding agreement, and she cannot claim the €4,000.  Moreover, he says that he relied on Alison’s agreement to accept €6,000 and entered into other contracts in the knowledge that he would not have to pay Alison the balance. Advise Alison on her legal position vis à vis (a) MD Ltd and (b) Peter.

questions.description: Barry collects old and unique comic books and graphic novels as a hobby. On Monday Barry’s friend Stacey phones him to a…
questions.text: Barry collects old and unique comic books and graphic novels as a hobby. On Monday Barry’s friend Stacey phones him to ask whether he would be interested in having a collection of old comic books she has found in her attic, as otherwise she “would just throw them out in the recycling”. Barry is delighted and immediately agrees to take the comics. When he collects the comics the next day Barry is so happy with them that he promises he will pay Stacey €200 for them when he gets his wages on Wednesday. Stacey is delighted by this and in the knowledge that she will get €200 on Wednesday she goes out for an expensive dinner on Monday night. However, when Barry is paid on Wednesday he does not give Stacey any money, and Stacey is now threatening to sue him for the €200. On Thursday morning Barry sees an advertisement in a local paper which states:”Limited Time Offer: Early Edition Copy of “Superman” for €50. If interested email Rebecca at comicgeek@email.ie by close of business Thursday” Barry decides that this would be a good addition to his comic book collection, so on Thursday he sends an email to Rebecca to say he is interested in buying the comic. Unfortunately he sends the email in such a hurry that he accidentally says he will pay €500 for the comic. Rebecca immediately replies saying that she accepts his offer to pay €500 for the comic book. Rebecca is now saying that they have a contract, and that Barry must pay her €500 for the comic book. Barry says that Rebecca must have known it was a mistake and that the comic book is not worth more than €50. Advise Barry on his legal position vis à vis (a) Stacey and (b) Rebecca

questions.description: In June 2010 Charles wins the 10 kilometre race at the Irish National Indoor Cycling Championships. After his win he tak…
questions.text: In June 2010 Charles wins the 10 kilometre race at the Irish National Indoor Cycling Championships. After his win he takes part in a national radio interview in which he says that he is “looking forward to competing in the 2012 Olympics in London”. The next month he enters into a sponsorship contract with a large Irish sports company, Megasports Ltd, whereby Megasports Ltd agree to pay Charles €15,000 per year five years if he appears in their advertising campaigns and wears their logo on his cycling clothes and cycling helmet when he competes. After signing the contract, Charles features in a television advertisement for Megasports Ltd which shows him cycling past a large mock signpost to “London”. However, in 2012 Charles is so outraged by the level of homelessness in London that he refuses to compete in the 2012 Olympic games as a measure of protest. Megasports Ltd is furious as its managing director, Tom, says the company only sponsored Charles because Tom heard his radio interview in September 2010 and assumed he would compete in the 2012 Olympics. He says that Charles misrepresenented his position both in the radio interview and by taking part in the television advertisement in which he was seen cycling in the direction of London. In addition, when Charles competed at the French Open Indoor Cycling Championships in January 2012 he is prevented by new competition rules, introduced in 2011, from wearing the Megasports Ltd logo on his helmet and cycling clothes. Megasports Ltd contacts Charles to say that it considers this a breach of the sponsorship contract. Megasports Ltd is threatening to stop its sponsorship of Charles and to sue Charles for misrepresentation and breach of contract. Advise Charles.

questions.description: John is a plumber who specialises in the supply and installation of bathroom equipment such as showers, baths and Jacuzz…
questions.text: John is a plumber who specialises in the supply and installation of bathroom equipment such as showers, baths and Jacuzzis. Last June John entered into a contract with Niamh, whereby John agreed to supply and install a Jacuzzi three meters long and one metre deep in Niamh’s bathroom, at a cost of €2,000, the full amount to be paid on 1 October. However, when the Jacuzzi is installed, Niamh is annoyed because it is only two and a half meters long and ninety centimetres deep. Other than the difference in size, the Jacuzzi is perfectly useable and has all the features Niamh requires. Niamh believes that John is in breach of contract and refuses to pay John the €2,000. In addition she wants damages of €3,000 to cover the cost of tearing out the Jacuzzi that has been installed and replacing it with a properly installed, bigger Jacuzzi and another €2,000 for the stress and inconvenience of getting the Jacuzzi replaced. However, John says the difference in size of the Jacuzzi is only minor, and that the Jacuzzi installed is still worth at least €1,750. He suspects that Niamh has no intention of actually replacing the installed Jacuzzi but instead wants to use the money to go on holidays. Advise John on (a) whether he is in breach of contract and (b) assuming John is in breach of contract, whether Niamh has to pay the €2,000 for the work done and (c) assuming John is in breach of contract, the extent to which Niamh will be successful in claiming damages.

questions.description: Ann is an actress who agrees to play the part of a prostitute in a new film Pretty Girl. The film features many graphic …
questions.text: Ann is an actress who agrees to play the part of a prostitute in a new film Pretty Girl. The film features many graphic sex scenes and nudity, and is banned by censors in several countries. Even though the film is not banned in Ireland it is never shown in Irish cinemas, although a small number of DVDs of the film are sold from sex shops. The film ends up making a loss, and the film producer, Clive is now refusing to pay Ann, claiming that that the contract is unenforceable as it is immoral and against public policy. Ann is finding it difficult to get another acting job in Ireland, so she decides to sell her car to make some money. On Monday she contacts Declan, a local car dealer, to discuss the possible purchase of her car. He promises to pay Ann €2,000 for the car, payment upon delivery. Ann is delighted and promises to deliver the car the following Friday. However, the next day Declan phones Ann to inform her that he has changed his mind as he has too many second hand cars in stock already. He claims that as the offer was a unilateral one and she has not yet performed “her part of the deal” or provided any consideration, he is entitled to do so. Ann wants to sue Declan for breach of contract. Advise Ann on her legal position vis à vis (a) Clive and (b) Declan.

questions.description: Catherine is starting up a catering business and wants to enter into a contract for the supply and installation of four …
questions.text: Catherine is starting up a catering business and wants to enter into a contract for the supply and installation of four new ovens.  In order to get the best deal possible, she puts a call for tenders in the local paper.  The advertisement states that the lowest or any bid will not necessarily be accepted.  Niamh and Seán both send in tenders on time.  Niamh’s tender states that she can provide and install the ovens for €6,000.  Seán’s tender states that he can provide and install the ovens for €6,500 “or €500 less than any lower bid”. Catherine awards the contract to Seán, on the basis that he will do the work for €5,500. Niamh is furious when she discovers that her tender was unsuccessful, and is threatening to sue Catherine. A month before the catering business is due to open Catherine realises that she needs additional financial support if the business is to succeed. She enters into an agreement with a local food supplier, Thomas, who agrees to invest €10,000 in the business in return for a promise that Catherine will enter into a contract with him for the supply of all foodstuffs to the business in the next two years. However, after two weeks of discussions they fail to agree on important terms of the supply contract, including the price and delivery arrangements. Catherine wants to end negotiations, but Thomas is insisting that she is under contractual duty to negotiate the contract. Advise Catherine.

questions.description: Sinead owns a hairdressing salon in Cork. Last October she decided to expand her business and she hired Pat to build a f…
questions.text: Sinead owns a hairdressing salon in Cork. Last October she decided to expand her business and she hired Pat to build a fully completed extension on to the salon. The contract between Sinead and Pat clearly state that the work was to be finished by January 31st 2011 and a penalty clause stated if the work was not completed by this date that Pat was liable to pay €500 per day until the extension was fully complete. In November, Pat entered into a sub-contract with Charles, a local plumber in the town. Charles agreed to carry out all the plumbing work on the extension by January 15th 2011 at an agreed price of €5,000. At the time of contracting, Pat emphasised that it was essential that this work be done on time, but he did not mention the penalty clause. In mid-December 2010 Charles contacted Pat to say that the work was taking longer than he had anticipated because of the cold weather. He said that he was going to need an extra payment of €2,000 to enable him to hire extra workers so he could finish the job on time. Pat reluctantly agreed to pay the money and Charles managed to finish on time. However once the work was done Pat wrote Charles a letter demanding the return of the additional €2,000 paid, stating that it was not part of the original agreed price and that Charles had “done noting extra for the cash”. In addition Sinead is unhappy with the quality of some of Charles ‘plumbing work. She claims that the water pressure is too low for washing clients’ hair and that there is a major leek from one of the sinks. She is threatening to sue Charles for breach of contract. Advise Charles on his legal position Vis a Vis (a) Pat and (b) Sinead.

questions.description: Jack is a 70 year old farmer who lives on his own in a rural area in the west of Ireland. He is in ill health and quite …
questions.text: Jack is a 70 year old farmer who lives on his own in a rural area in the west of Ireland. He is in ill health and quite dependent on his neighbour, Sheila, who regularly visits him and assists him with basic household jobs such as cooking and cleaning. Sheila minds Jack’s bank account books for him and occasionally drives him to his local bank. One day when Sheila drives Jack to his local bank Jack transfers €20,000 from his personal savings account to Sheila’s bank account. Jack does not discuss the transaction in any detail with any bank representative: nor does he receive any independent legal advice on the transaction. Two months later Sheila approaches Jack and asks him to sign a petition to stop the closure of a local school. Jack doesn’t have his reading glasses and so he signs the petition without reading it. Jack’s nephew Colin visits Jack later that year and upon investigation discovers that the “petition” is, in fact a bank guarantee form which guarantees Sheila’s depts. Up to €25,000. Colin is concerned that his uncle may become liable under this guarantee and wants to know if there is any way the guarantee may be void. He also wants to know if the transfer of €20,000 to Sheila can be set aside. Advise Colin.

questions.description: Eamon is the owner of a recording studio. He rents out his recording studio to independent musicians for a fee of €100 p…
questions.text: Eamon is the owner of a recording studio. He rents out his recording studio to independent musicians for a fee of €100 per day. On 15th May he agreed to hire out his studio for a 2 week period to Carla. The contract stated that the recording was to take place from 1st June to 14th June and that Carla was to pay a deposit of €500 on 15th May and the balance €900 on the 14th June. Carla paid the deposit on 15th May. However on 31st May, the day before recording was due to begin, Carla got laryngitis and lost her voice. She went to the doctor who told her that she would not be able to sing for at least two weeks. Carla is claiming that the contract is frustrated and that not only does she does not have to pay the €900 due, but also that she is entitled to the return of her €500 deposit. Eamon wants Carla to pay the remaining €900 due especially as he cannot hire out the recording studio to anybody else at such short notice. Eamon also occasionally acts as an agent for up and coming musicians and singers. Three years he agreed to act as an agent for Hannah, a 15 year old singer at the start of her career. The contract stated that Eamon was to receive a commission of 20% of all Hannah’s income, from music or related merchandise, for the next 10 years. However, now Hannah is 18 she has decided that she is unhappy with the arrangement with Eamon, and she has told Eamon that she wants to cancel their contract and look for a better deal with another agent. Eamon wants to sue Hannah for breach of contract. Advise Eamon on his legal position Vis a Vis (a) Carla and (b) Hannah

questions.description: On January 1st, Eoin places an advertisement in the Irish Times offering to sell his car for €3,000 “on a first come fir…
questions.text: On January 1st, Eoin places an advertisement in the Irish Times offering to sell his car for €3,000 “on a first come first served basis” and providing his postal and his email address.  Jenny replies that evening by post saying that she is interested in the car but asking whether Eoin might be prepared to accept a lower price and stating that “€2,750 would seem correct”.  Although this is the only response Eoin has to his advertisement, he ignores it.  On January 14th, Jenny emails Eoin agreeing to pay €3,000.  On the same day and prior to reading his emails, Eoin brings the car to an auction run by Cars-or-us Ltd which advertises to potential customers that all cars will be sold “without reserve”.  Carrie is the only bidder for Eoin’s car and she bids €2,000.  Eoin instructs the auctioneer to withdraw the car from the auction before the hammer falls.  He then sells the car privately to Martin for €2,800.  Jenny and Carrie both announce their intention to sue Eoin for breach of contract. Advise Eoin.

questions.description: Frank, a seventeen year old student from Galway has just accepted a place in UCD.  He travels by train from Galway to Du…
questions.text: Frank, a seventeen year old student from Galway has just accepted a place in UCD.  He travels by train from Galway to Dublin, his first ever train trip.  On his return at the end of the week to Galway, he decides to leave his bag which contains a laptop computer in the cloakroom at the train station in Dublin.  The cloakroom is operated by the railway company and is free to customers. He gives the bag to Jill, the cloakroom attendant, and is handed a ticket in return.  As he is late for his train, he puts this into his pocket without reading it.  Jill carelessly leaves the cloakroom unlocked and unattended while she takes a 30 minute lunch break.  On her return she finds that Fran’s bag has been stolen.  When Frank is notified, he decides to sue the railway company for breach of contract.  The railway company does not deny negligence but points to a clause on the back of Frank’s ticket and which is also displayed inside the carriages on all their trains.  This states that the railway company is not liable for “any act or omission” of the company. Advise Frank as to whether the clause is incorporated into the contract and, if it is, whether it covers the railway company’s negligence.  Also advise Frank as to the effect, if any, of his age on the contract.

questions.description: Ted runs his own taxi business.  He is approached by Bill, a lawyer, who wants to transport Mr Finny, his goldfish, from…
questions.text: Ted runs his own taxi business.  He is approached by Bill, a lawyer, who wants to transport Mr Finny, his goldfish, from a veterinarian clinic in Navan to his house in Dundalk.  Bill agrees to pay Ted €100 for the journey and he pays a deposit of €25.  When Ted and Mr Finny arrive in Dundalk, Bill claims that he has learned that Ted’s licence precludes him from transporting animals (apart from guide-dogs) or fish.  He refuses to pay and asks for the return of the deposit. Ted then sells one of his fleet of taxis to Maire. He tells her that the model is in “perfect condition” and is a 2008 model.  It subsequently transpires that it is a 2001 model and the roof leaks.  It also fails its NCT because it is unroadworthy. Maire claims that both representations made by Ted constitute terms of the contract. Advise Ted.

questions.description: Chuck is employed for three months to canvass for Shane, a politician in the local elections.  One month after he commen…
questions.text: Chuck is employed for three months to canvass for Shane, a politician in the local elections.  One month after he commences work, he becomes embroiled in a heated argument with a member of the public on the topic of bank nationalizations.  Chuck throws a punch and is arrested for assault.  Despite arguing provocation, he is convicted and sentenced to one month in prison.  While in prison, Chuck is approached by Len, a fellow inmate, who is seeking to locate an apartment to live in upon his imminent release.  He has heard that Chuck’s current tenant has just left.  Len is finishing serving a ten year sentence for grievous bodily harm and suggests that it would be in Chuck’s “best interests” to lease him the apartment for six months.  Chuck reluctantly agrees and a rent is agreed which is 10% above the rate paid by the previous tenant.  Upon leaving prison, Chuck phones Shane to tell him that he is ready to complete the last month of his contract but he is informed that the contract is frustrated. Chuck seeks your advice in relation to his contract with Shane and also as to whether the contract with Len is voidable or invalid.

questions.description: In January 2000, Fay rents an office from Well Ltd agreeing to pay an annual rent of €55,000 subject to a rent review on…
questions.text: In January 2000, Fay rents an office from Well Ltd agreeing to pay an annual rent of €55,000 subject to a rent review on 30th December 2009.  The rent review clause provides that on that date the rent should be increased to the current market rental value as agreed between the parties – save that in no circumstances should the rent be lower than the figure payable immediately before the review.  In December, Max, a senior manager in Well Ltd writes to Fay suggesting a figure of €52,000 for the new rent.  Fay writes back readily agreeing to renew upon the terms proposed.  Max then realises his mistake and writes to Fay explaining that he had intended to propose a rent of €95,000 and asking her to treat his letter as if it had suggested a review at that figure.  Fay informs Max that she will not agree to the correction proposed and that she considers that a binding agreement exists.  While accepting that Max has authority to act on its behalf, Well Ltd argues that Max’s mistake invalidates the agreement.  Well Ltd is unhappy with Max’s performance and it decides his annual salary of €50,000 could be put to better use.  In February 2010, Max is dismissed and Well Ltd pays him compensation of €150,000.  In April 2010, it is discovered that Max has engaged in insider dealing in breach of his service contract and in breach of company law.  It becomes clear thus that at the time it terminated his contract, Well Ltd could have dismissed Max for breach without having to pay compensation.  Well Ltd argues that the termination contract is void and the compensation is repayable. Advise Well Ltd.

questions.description: Sadhbh, one of the largest suppliers of homemade confectionery in Ireland, has been supplying homemade fudge to Mally & …
questions.text: Sadhbh, one of the largest suppliers of homemade confectionery in Ireland, has been supplying homemade fudge to Mally & Sullivan Ltd (M&S) since 1980.  In order to ensure flexibility in its dealings with suppliers, M&S deliberately abstains from concluding any express contract or contracts with its suppliers.  Thus, there is no written contract between M&S and Sadhbh.  The practice has been for M&S to order fudge, normally six weeks in advance, by a computerised process which specifies the quantities and flavours required.  A price is then agreed on the telephone.  In the course of establishing, maintaining and conducting its relationship with her, Sadhbh argues that M&S implicitly promised and induced her to believe the relationship would be a long term one and would be terminable only upon reasonable notice; and that during the subsistence of the relationship M&S would order fudge in quantities and at prices which in all the circumstances were reasonable.  However, in January 2010, M&S terminates the arrangement without prior notice.  Sadhbh argues that an implied contract exists requiring M&S to give reasonable notice of termination.  She also argues that M&S is estopped from terminating the arrangement without reasonable notice.  M&S rejects both claims and argues that there was no intention to create a contract and that the alleged obligation to acquire fudge from Sadbhb was insufficiently certain to found any contractual obligation because there were no objective criteria by which a court could assess what would be reasonable either as to quantity of price. Advise Sadbhb.

questions.description: Fred operates a plant hire business.  He leases a site from Ken intending to use it for recycling and storing bulk mater…
questions.text: Fred operates a plant hire business.  He leases a site from Ken intending to use it for recycling and storing bulk materials.  The lease provides that Fred may “use the premises only for the storage of bulk road materials or for such other purposes as may from time to time be approved in writing by the Landlords”.  After using this site for recycling operations for one month, noise and dust from his operations lead to complaints and the local council serves an enforcement notice on Fred and Ken Ltd ordering them to cease using the site for the recycling of construction and demolition materials on the grounds that this violates existing planning laws.  Fred claims that the lease has been frustrated as the order renders illegal the only use of the site which was permitted under the lease and/or the only use of relevance to him.  He claims that the property is of no value to him now and that due to the property crisis, the likelihood of sub-letting the site is minimal. Fred had hired twelve workers from India for one year intending to use them to carry out this specialist recycling work until they trained local workers.  Although Fred finds work for these workers for four weeks with an associated company, he then claims that no further work is available and that his contracts with them are frustrated.  He also notes that one of the workers, Sonia, is only seventeen and thus, irrespective of the frustration, he argues that his contract with her would be void on this ground. Advise Fred.

questions.description: On Monday, Chuck phones Sharon, a car dealer, to discuss the possible purchase of her Aston Martin car.  He promises to …
questions.text: On Monday, Chuck phones Sharon, a car dealer, to discuss the possible purchase of her Aston Martin car.  He promises to pay Sharon €120,000 for the car, payment upon delivery.  Sharon is delighted and promises to deliver the car the following Friday.  However, the next day, Chuck phones Sharon to inform her that his new age spiritual councillor Mo has advised him that his interest in the car is pathetic and part of a mid-life crisis.  As a consequence he tells Sharon that he wishes to revoke his offer.  He claims that as the offer was a unilateral one and she has not yet performed “her part of the deal” or provided any consideration, he is entitled to do so.  Sharon announces her intention to sue him for breach of contract. Chuck, again on Mo’s advice, agrees to transfer ownership of his highly successful PR company to Mo believing that it will be used to help Mo to disseminate his message to a wider audience.  Chuck is advised on the transaction by Hugh, a solicitor who is Mo’s neighbour.  One week after the transfer is complete, Chuck decides that he has been unwise and seeks to avoid the agreement with Mo. Advise Chuck.

questions.description: Joe manages a small advertising company.  He is concerned that one of his employees, Caroline, has been absent for a sig…
questions.text: Joe manages a small advertising company.  He is concerned that one of his employees, Caroline, has been absent for a significant number of days each month due to illness.  He determines that her health would improve if she gave up smoking.  In order not to single Caroline out, he writes to all staff promising them €1,000 if they do not smoke for six months.  Hugh, a new recruit and a smoker, discusses the offer with Caroline and they both agree to quit smoking.  Three weeks later, Joe learns that Hugh is intent on earning the €1,000 and he writes again to all staff stating that the offer is only open to employees who have been with the company for more than a year. Hugh ignores this and continues to desist from smoking.  At the end of the six month period, both Caroline and Hugh demand their €1,000 from Joe.  Joe argues that no contract exists.  He claims the promise was not an offer and that even if it was that neither party informed him of their intended acceptance or provided consideration.  He also argues that if an offer was made, he revoked the offer before Hugh accepted. Advise Hugh and Caroline.

questions.description: Redmond advertises in the Irish Times seeking a tenant for a business premises he owns adjacent to his house in the cent…
questions.text: Redmond advertises in the Irish Times seeking a tenant for a business premises he owns adjacent to his house in the centre of Dublin. Jane responds to the advertisement and arranges to inspect the premises.  One year earlier Jane, who was then known as Jane Robinson, was convicted of permitting disorderly conduct at a café in Wicklow. Jane subsequently changed her name by deed poll to Jane Potter and she introduces herself to Redmond thus.  She agrees with Redmond to lease the property for three years. Redmond then learns Jane’s true identity and argues that he remembers the conviction of Jane Robinson and that he would not have leased the property to Jane if he had been aware of this fact.  He argues that the mistake is void as he was mistaken with regard to Jane’s identity. Redmond refuses to hand over the keys of the premises to Jane. Advise Jane.

questions.description: Peter is the owner of a large farm in Cork.  A man of few words, he is very private and has a very small circle of frien…
questions.text: Peter is the owner of a large farm in Cork.  A man of few words, he is very private and has a very small circle of friends.  In 1985 after Peter’s wife dies, Peter becomes very depressed and his neighbour’s son Jimmy starts helping him on the farm.  Jimmy undertakes both physical work and book-keeping tasks for Peter, contributing a huge amount of help over the next 20 years and frequently working 18-hour days, 7 days a week.  Jimmy is not paid and survives on an allowance from his parents.  His hope is that he will inherit the farm.  In 2005 his hope develops into an expectation when Peter hands Jimmy a cheque stating ‘That’s for my death duties’.  In the following years Peter encourages Jimmy’s expectation by making indirect remarks which could only suggest that Jimmy would continue to be involved with the farm after Peter’s death. While no direct conversation is ever held on the subject of the inheritance, in 2008 Peter makes a will leaving the farm to Jimmy. However, in 2009, Peter has an argument with Jimmy and destroys the will.  Two weeks later, Peter dies without leaving a will and his estate is passed on intestacy to his siblings.  Jimmy announces his intention to bring an action in estoppel. Advise Peter’s siblings.

questions.description: Ben groups hops which he uses to make herbal medicines. Kelly approaches Ben with a view to buying hops to make her very…
questions.text: Ben groups hops which he uses to make herbal medicines. Kelly approaches Ben with a view to buying hops to make her very popular beer. Kelly asks if the hops have been treated with sulphur stating that she would not even discuss the price if they have. Ben assures her that they have not.  Kelly is delighted and they discuss the price and delivery details.  Ben then presents her with a document which he describes as “my conditions of sale”.  A clause in the document states as follows: “In the event of any hops sold not complying with the express terms of the contract of sale, we will refund all payments made to us by the buyer and this shall be the limit of our obligation.  We hereby exclude all liability for any loss or damage arising from the use of any hops supplied by us and for any consequential loss or damage arising out of such use.” Kelly glances at the document but does not read through the terms. She agrees to buy the hops.  Later, after she has made the beer, Kelly discovers that sulphur was used on a small portion of the hops. As a result, Kelly has to dispose of her entire production of beer suffering significant loss.  When she announces her intention to sue Ben for breach of contract, he argues that the statement about the sulphur was a mere representation not a term. He also claims that whether it was a representation or a term, the limitation clause covers the event which has occurred and that Kelly is merely entitled to a refund. Advise Kelly on Ben’s chances of succeeding with these arguments.
