questions.description: Bob is delighted that his niece Marie and her boyfriend Mick have become engaged.  He has been encouraging the couple to…
questions.text: Bob is delighted that his niece Marie and her boyfriend Mick have become engaged.  He has been encouraging the couple to get married for months and promising them that he will “pay for the wedding” if they do so.  Bob also takes out a mortgage on a house and promises the couple that the house will be theirs if they complete the mortgage payments. The couple organise a lavish reception at a cost of €500,000 and Marie buys a €40,000 designer wedding dress.  After the wedding, the couple move into the house and make the first mortgage payment. They also send Bob a bill for €540,000 to cover the cost of the reception and Marie’s dress. Bob is incandescent with rage.  He complains that he did not intend to pay for such an expensive wedding and that they have taken advantage of his generosity.  He argues that they have no contractual entitlement to his money.  He also demands that they vacate the house. Marie and Mick seek your advice.

questions.description: In January, Kevin books a two week holiday for himself and his family with Sunnyair Ltd.  He chooses a particular packag…
questions.text: In January, Kevin books a two week holiday for himself and his family with Sunnyair Ltd.  He chooses a particular package on the basis of Sunnyair Ltd’s brochure which promises a comfortable hotel with excellent food, a children’s club, a swimming pool and push chairs available for children.  Kevin arrives at the hotel in June and discovers that the food is not to his liking, the pool has been drained, no children’s club is available and push chairs can only be hired from a local shop.  Sunnyair Ltd argues that the statements in the brochure constitute statements of future intention and do not form part of the contract.  It also argues that the statements concerning the food, pool and push chairs were factually accurate. Kevin seeks your advice on suing Sunnyair for misrepresentation and/or breach of contract.  He wishes to recover for the loss suffered by himself and his family.

questions.description: In early 2008, Sean enters into a contract to sell a plot of land to Liam for €2 million.  Liam intends to develop the l…
questions.text: In early 2008, Sean enters into a contract to sell a plot of land to Liam for €2 million.  Liam intends to develop the land which has been used for nearly 20 years as a school playing pitch and build a block of apartments.  Unknown to either party, a sewage pipe crosses the land removing raw sewage from a neighbouring block of flats owned by Dublin Corporation.  This is discovered by Liam who is advised that he cannot move the pipe and that incorporating the pipe in his development plans will necessitate further costs of €1 million.  Furthermore, it will delay completion of the development by six months.  Liam’s other problem is that since the contract has been entered the property market has crashed and he is advised that the market value of his property is only about €500,000. Sean seeks an order of specific performance and Liam asks your advice about avoiding the contract on grounds of mistake or frustration.

questions.description: Mary and Felicity use their joint savings to buy a house in Tralee owned by Fiona.  Mary and Felicity agree among themse…
questions.text: Mary and Felicity use their joint savings to buy a house in Tralee owned by Fiona.  Mary and Felicity agree among themselves that the title should be registered in Mary’s name only in order not to jeopardise Felicity’s social welfare payments which are means tested.  Both Felicity and Mary plan to live in the house and contribute towards the cost of running the house.  The contract provides that the purchase is subject to Mary’s surveyor confirming that the house is sound and in good condition.  Fiona then changes her mind and refuses to proceed with the sale.  She denies Mary’s surveyor access to the property and argues that as a consequence a condition for sale cannot be fulfilled.  She also argues that the contract is void on grounds of illegality. Advise Mary and Felicity.

questions.description: Joe applies for admission to University College Dublin (“UCD”) and in January 2006 is immediately granted a place on a c…
questions.text: Joe applies for admission to University College Dublin (“UCD”) and in January 2006 is immediately granted a place on a course “Law for Non-Lawyers” commencing in September 2006.  Although traditionally, the pass mark for the examination was 40%, in April 2006 UCD amends its regulations changing the pass mark to 50%.  An erratum slip to this effect is included in almost all the copies of the Course manual handed out to students upon their arrival in September 2006.  Although Joe commences the course in September 2006, he claims that his manual did not contain an erratum slip.  Notices explaining the change are also posted on the Course’s notice board and students are repeatedly advised of the change in classes during the academic year.  In his summer 2007 examinations, Joe obtains a mark of 48% and is recorded as failing the course.  Joe argues that the contract was formed in January 2006 and the terms of that contract are those contained in the manual handed to him upon commencement of the course in September 2006 and do not include any alterations thereof which were not brought to his attention at that time.  He also argues that he had an expectation that no changes would be made to the regulations. Advise UCD.

questions.description: A representative of Reillyair approaches Ciara as she is boarding her flight from Dublin to Manchester with her boyfrien…
questions.text: A representative of Reillyair approaches Ciara as she is boarding her flight from Dublin to Manchester with her boyfriend Clive.  Ciara, a model, is informed that she might be the millionth passenger and asked if she would be prepared to participate in publicity exercises to mark the occasion of the use of Reillyair’s one millionth ticket to Manchester. When she agrees, Ciara is announced publicly as the millionth passenger.  She is informed that she will have unlimited travel for life on any Reillyair flight and a chosen nominee will have free flights for life on the Dublin to Manchester route. The press photos show Ciara holding ticket number 1,000,000.  Six months later, Clive, whose relationship with Ciara has ended, realises that it was his ticket that was numbered 1,000,000.  Clive suspects that Ciara was approached as she was more photogenic of the two and she was given his ticket to hold for publicity purposes. He informs Reillyair that he is the rightful recipient of the offer and that he is entitled to flights for life.  In the meantime, Reillyair decides that the Dublin to Manchester route is not sufficiently lucrative and it drops this route from its schedule. Reillyair also seeks to restrict Ciara’s flights by allowing her to travel only as a standby passenger. Ciara and her mother Mable (her chosen nominee) sue Reillyair for breach of contract.  Reillyair argues that no enforceable contract exists with Ciara and that even if it does Mable is not a party to this contract and cannot enforce it. Advise Reillyair.

questions.description: Keelin is an internationally recognised collector of Waterford crystal.  She reads in the “Collector” of an auction of “…
questions.text: Keelin is an internationally recognised collector of Waterford crystal.  She reads in the “Collector” of an auction of “fine crystal” in Dublin and attends the viewing the day before the auction.  There, she meets Eoin, a recently retired glass-blower in the Waterford crystal factory.  Eoin explains his background and tells her that in order to bolster his benison he has decided to sell his entire personal collection of glasses in one lot, Lot 17.  Keelin inspects Lot 17 and is delighted with the quality of the collection.  She is unaware that at least half of the glasses are not Waterford crystal but are imitations mass produced in China.  Eoin’s son bought them for Eoin as a joke during one of his trips to the East.  Eoin is aware of Keelin’s mistake but ensures that he does not offer more than “fine crystal” in general terms.  When Keelin tells him sympathetically that it must be hard to part with “his life’s work”, Eoin responds merely by saying “No, it is not”.  Keelin buys Lot 17 which is advertised merely as “Assorted Glassware”.  Keelin also bids €200 for a Waterford crystal chandelier owned by Tom.  Although the chandelier is advertised as “without reserve”, it is withdrawn from the auction when hers is the only bid. The auctioneer is acting on Tom’s instructions. Two years later, Keelin reads that the chandelier has been sold at Christies for €40,000.  She wishes to sue Tom for breach of contract.  Keelin also discovers the true origins of the Chinese glasses and claims that either the contract with Eoin is invalid as a result of this mistake and that she is entitled to her money back or that Eoin is liable for failing to disclose the fact that the glasses were not all Waterford crystal. Advise Keelin.

questions.description: Sean D. Builders Ltd (“SDB”) enters into a contract to extend a house in Cork owned by Brian.  When the work is complete…
questions.text: Sean D. Builders Ltd (“SDB”) enters into a contract to extend a house in Cork owned by Brian.  When the work is completed, Brian claims that as planning permission has been refused for the extension, the contract is unenforceable on the grounds of illegality.  Brian refuses to pay the agreed sum. SDB learns that Brian has never sought planning permission and it sues Brian for breach of contract. SDB then enters into a contract with Cork County Council to build 100 units of affordable housing.  Subsequently the County Council inform SDB that the contract has been frustrated. The County Council claim that the contract has been rendered impossible to perform due to the refusal of the Government to provide them with the necessary funds.In order to balance the budget in recessionary times, the County Council claim that money previously designated for building projects has to be used to meet expenses in three of its hospitals. Advise SDB.

questions.description: Paddy plans to visit New York and checks the prices on the Air Dublin website.  He sees a fare advertised on the site as…
questions.text: Paddy plans to visit New York and checks the prices on the Air Dublin website.  He sees a fare advertised on the site as a “€15.99 Summer Special” for a business class fare to New York.  He immediately books this flight giving his credit card details and he receives an automatic email from Air Dublin confirming the flight.  Paddy immediately books a hotel room in New York, his credit card being automatically debited by the hotel’s website which has a “no refunds” policy.  Three hours later, Paddy receives a call from Sheila, Air Dublin’s Sales Manager, explaining that the price on the website was incorrect and was part of a planned promotional campaign which had not yet been approved.  Sheila informs him that the fare should not have been advertised on the site and that the fare would not be charged to his credit card.  When Paddy insists that a valid contract exists, Sheila denies that this is the case.  She argues that any contract which did exist would be “void as a result of the very obvious mistake”. She states that “no sensible person would expect to get a business class fare for €15.99 when the normal price was €1,599”. Advise Paddy.

questions.description: Isobel, a sixteen year old aspiring model, enters into a contract with a modelling agency, Models Ltd.  The contract inv…
questions.text: Isobel, a sixteen year old aspiring model, enters into a contract with a modelling agency, Models Ltd.  The contract involves a six month training course and a seven month placement with Jim Roka, a well known dress designer.  (Models Ltd enters into a separate agreement with Jim agreeing to supply a trained model, Isobel, for seven months for €10,000).  Anna, the managing director of Models Inc explains that while Isobel will not have to pay for the training course she will not receive any payment for the placement.  The contract also provides that Isobel will be required to give Models Inc any money that she earns independently from working as a model during the duration of the contract.  Isobel verbally agrees.  Thirteen months later, when the placement is complete, Anna learns that Isobel has not disclosed over €5,000 which she received for modelling for a newspaper.  Isobel refuses to hand over this money arguing that her contract was invalid and/or unenforceable on the grounds of incapacity, undue influence and failure to comply with formal requirements. Advise Models Ltd.

questions.description: Enda enters into an understanding with his niece Grainne allowing her to reside in an apartment owned by Enda free of ch…
questions.text: Enda enters into an understanding with his niece Grainne allowing her to reside in an apartment owned by Enda free of charge “while studying for the Law Society entrance examinations”.  Grainne is a law graduate who lives on a monthly allowance of €1,000 from her deceased mother’s estate.  Enda explains to Grainne that it was her mother’s dearest wish that she become a solicitor and that he wants to help her achieve this goal.  He also promises that when she qualifies as a solicitor, he will give her the freehold interest in the property.  Four years later, Grainne is still claiming to be studying for the examinations but has yet to pass a single subject.  Enda overhears her telling a friend that she plans to continue to stay in the apartment indefinitely and that as long as she spends “a few hours a year studying” that she cannot be asked to leave.  Infuriated, Enda seeks to evict Grainne claiming that she has not provided consideration for his promise to allow her to reside in the property and that it lacks certainty and intent.  Enda also claims that as Grainne has not yet accepted his offer of the freehold interest in the property that he is entitled to withdraw his offer. Advise Grainne.

questions.description: On Monday, Loman shows Peter a van he is selling and informs him that there is only 30,000 kilometres on the odometer.  …
questions.text: On Monday, Loman shows Peter a van he is selling and informs him that there is only 30,000 kilometres on the odometer.  Neither Loman nor Peter is involved in the motor trade and neither has any special knowledge of mechanics.  After a rudimentary inspection, Peter agrees to buy the van.  Unknown to either, the van’s log book and odometer have been altered by a previous owner to hide the fact that the van has completed 230,000 kilometres.  Peter plans to use the van to perform a contract with Matt, an artist, to deliver two paintings from Dublin to London for an auction the following Friday.  Unfortunately, due to its ancient gearbox, the van breaks down on Wednesday on route to the ferry port in Cork.  Peter thus misses the daily sailing to Pembroke.  On Thursday, a storm prevents any ferries from sailing.  Peter is quoted €400 for a return flight from Dublin to London but decides this is too expensive.  Instead, Peter travels on the ferry early on Friday morning to Pembroke and takes the express train from Pembroke to London.  Unfortunately, he arrives in London on Friday evening too late for the auction.  Having been forced to withdraw the paintings, Matt sues Peter for breach of contract.  Peter claims that the contract with Matt was frustrated but that if it was not, only nominal charges are due.  Peter then initiates a claim against Loman for breach of contract. Advise Peter.

questions.text: Kevin advertises in the “Independent” the sale of his family silver.  The advertisement states a price of “€3,000 or nearest offer for the lot”.  He receives a response from Carrie in New York who claims to be a second cousin-once-removed of his.  Carrie writes a passionate letter explaining that she wants to “get in touch with her roots” and is anxious to acquire all the silver.  She indicates her willingness to pay €3,000 and informs him that she will collect it on her forthcoming visit to Ireland.  Despite a number of other matching offers, Kevin, by post, agrees to sell the silver to her but when Carrie arrives at his door, she asks whether he’ll take a cheque.  Reluctantly but on the basis that she looks like his Aunt, Kevin takes the cheque.  It subsequently turns out that Carrie is not a relation of his at all.  She absconds and the cheque is rejected by the bank. Kevin discovers that she has sold the silver to an antique dealer.  He seeks your advice about his chances of getting the silver back.

questions.text: Finn books Joe’s theatre to host a concert for his wine club.  Finn informs Joe of his plans to seat 500 people for the event and that he intends to provide food and wine to the entire audience.  Joe accepts a €1,000 deposit and they agree that the remaining €3,000 will be paid on the morning of the concert. Two days before the concert, Finn’s application for a wine licence for the event is turned down by the District Court on the grounds that Finn had not completed all the relevant paperwork to accompany the application.  Finn tells Joe that their contact is frustrated and that he is treating the contract as at an end. Joe disputes this and argues that the contract is valid but that he is treating Finn’s statement as a repudiatory breach. Advise Finn.

questions.description: Megan sues Fred to recover a debt of €2,000.  Fred sends a cheque to Megan for €500 indicating that he accepts liability…
questions.text: Megan sues Fred to recover a debt of €2,000.  Fred sends a cheque to Megan for €500 indicating that he accepts liability for this amount only and that the cheque is being sent “in full settlement of the dispute”.  He sends a copy of this letter to the court.  Megan cashes the cheque and spends the money but continues her action to recover the remainder.  Fred argues that Megan is contractually bound to abandon her claim or alternatively that she is stopped from continuing the action. Advise Megan.

questions.text: Sugary Cereals Ltd (“SCL”) operates a large manufacturing plant producing a range of popular breakfast cereals.  SCL is in dispute with a trade union representing its employees who claim that SCL is not paying appropriate holiday pay to its employees.  The union threaten to picket unless SCL agrees to enter into new contracts of employment with its employees on terms approved by the union.  As SCL has a large order to fill, it agrees to these new contracts and its employees commence to work on the new terms.  However, three months later, SCL claims that the new contracts are void on the grounds of duress.  At the same time, SCL is invoiced by its accountants for €14,000 for audit services provided. SCL’s managing director tells the accountants that unless this figure is substantially reduced, he will personally see to it that no company in the country will ever hire them again.  The accountants agree but subsequently argue that the agreement is void for duress. Advise SCL.
